content.text: I would like to check in this suitcase, and I have one {{c1::carry-on bag}} to bring into the cabin.
content.explanation: A carry-on bag (or hand luggage) is the small suitcase or backpack that passengers are permitted to bring onto the plane with them rather than checking it.
content.spanish: Me gustaría registrar esta maleta, y tengo una maleta de mano para llevar en la cabina.
content.usage:
Pattern: carry-on bag / carry-on luggage
  • Make sure your carry-on bag fits in the overhead bin.
  • I only travel with a carry-on bag to save time.

content.scenario: English Phonetics: Airport Gate Connected Speech ✈️
content.text: At the gate, the announcement "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" blends the last two words via consonant-to-consonant linking, making "in ten" sound like {{c1::"int-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]}}.
content.explanation: When a word ends in /n/ and the next begins with /t/, the alveolar nasal /n/ assimilates or links directly to the alveolar stop /t/ to allow smooth, rapid articulation, resulting in a single elongated nasal-to-stop transition.
content.spanish: En la puerta, el anuncio "We will begin boarding in ten minutes" fusiona las últimas dos palabras mediante enlace consonante-consonante, haciendo que "in ten" suene como "int-ten".
content.usage:
Phonetic Drill: in ten minutes → "in-ten" [ɪnˈtɛn]
Contrastive Practice: Pronounce both 'n' and 't' as a single continuous block without a glottal pause.

content.scenario: English Daily Life: Airport Baggage Claim ✈️
content.text: At the baggage claim desk, if your luggage is missing, you should report it politely saying: "Excuse me, my bag {{c1::didn't come out}} on the carousel, and I'd like to {{c2::file a claim}}."
content.explanation: To "file a claim" is the official terminology for registering a lost item report. Saying "didn't come out" is the natural phrasal expression for baggage that did not arrive.
content.spanish: En el mostrador de reclamo de equipaje, si tu maleta se perdió, debes reportarlo diciendo educadamente: "Excuse me, my bag didn't come out on the carousel, and I'd like to file a claim."
content.usage:
Pattern: file a claim / didn't come out on [carousel]
  • I need to file a claim for my damaged baggage.
  • My suitcase didn't come out on carousel 4.

content.text: Could you please tell me where the {{c1::baggage drop-off}} counter is?
content.explanation: The 'baggage drop-off' is the designated counter where passengers who have already checked in online can drop off their luggage before heading to security.
content.spanish: ¿Podría decirme por favor dónde está el mostrador de entrega de equipaje?
content.usage:
Pattern: baggage drop-off / bag drop
  • Where is the bag drop for airline passengers?
  • You can bypass the main line if you only need the baggage drop-off.

content.text: I need to check in my luggage, but I want to keep this briefcase as my {{c1::personal item}}.
content.explanation: A 'personal item' is a small bag (like a purse, laptop bag, or small backpack) that must fit under the seat in front of you on a plane, distinct from standard carry-on luggage.
content.spanish: Necesito registrar mi equipaje, pero quiero conservar este maletín como mi artículo personal.
content.usage:
Pattern: personal item
  • Most budget airlines allow one personal item for free.
  • Your laptop bag counts as a personal item.

content.text: The flight has been delayed, so we need to wait at the {{c1::departure gate}}.
content.explanation: The 'departure gate' (or boarding gate) is the specific area in an airport terminal where passengers assemble to board their aircraft.
content.spanish: El vuelo se ha retrasado, así que tenemos que esperar en la puerta de salida/embarque.
content.usage:
Pattern: departure gate / boarding gate
  • Our departure gate has changed from gate 12 to gate 15.
  • Please report to the departure gate immediately.

content.text: Excuse me, does this flight have a {{c1::layover}} in New York?
content.explanation: A 'layover' (or connection) is a period of waiting between flights at an intermediate airport before reaching the final destination.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿este vuelo tiene una escala en Nueva York?
content.usage:
Pattern: layover / connection
  • We have a three-hour layover in Chicago.
  • I prefer direct flights because I hate long layovers.

content.text: We need to go through {{c1::security screening}} before we can access the duty-free area.
content.explanation: The 'security screening' is the checkpoint in an airport where passengers and their baggage are inspected by security officers and scanning machines.
content.spanish: Necesitamos pasar por el control de seguridad antes de poder acceder al área libre de impuestos.
content.usage:
Pattern: security screening / security check
  • The line at the security screening was incredibly long.
  • Remove all laptops and liquids before security screening.

content.text: Please have your {{c1::boarding pass}} and ID ready for inspection.
content.explanation: A 'boarding pass' is a document provided by an airline during check-in, giving a passenger permission to board the airplane for a specific flight.
content.spanish: Por favor, tenga su pase de abordar e identificación listos para inspección.
content.usage:
Pattern: boarding pass / boarding document
  • You can show your digital boarding pass on your phone.
  • Please print your boarding pass at the kiosk.

content.text: My flight was overbooked, and they offered me travel vouchers if I {{c1::volunteered to take}} a later flight.
content.explanation: To 'volunteer to take' a later flight means agreeing to give up your seat on an overbooked flight in exchange for compensation (such as travel vouchers or money).
content.spanish: Mi vuelo estaba sobrevendido, y me ofrecieron vales de viaje si me ofrecía como voluntario para tomar un vuelo posterior.
content.usage:
Pattern: volunteer to take [flight]
  • No one volunteered to take the next flight, so they started bumped passengers.
  • I volunteered to take a later flight because the vouchers were worth $500.

content.text: Could you tell me which terminal is for {{c1::domestic flights}}?
content.explanation: 'Domestic flights' are flights that stay within the boundaries of the same country, as opposed to international flights.
content.spanish: ¿Podría decirme qué terminal es para vuelos nacionales?
content.usage:
Pattern: domestic flights / domestic terminal
  • All domestic flights depart from Terminal 2.
  • I am traveling within the country, so I need to check in for domestic flights.

content.text: I missed my flight; is there a desk where I can {{c1::rebook my ticket}}?
content.explanation: To 'rebook a ticket' means to change the reservation on a ticket, typically after missing a flight or if a flight is canceled, allocating the passenger to a new flight.
content.spanish: Perdí mi vuelo; ¿hay algún mostrador donde pueda volver a reservar mi boleto?
content.usage:
Pattern: rebook my ticket / rebook a flight
  • The airline helped me rebook my ticket for the next available flight.
  • Can I rebook my ticket online without paying a fee?

content.text: Please place all metal objects in the bin before going through the {{c1::metal detector}}.
content.explanation: A 'metal detector' is the electronic security portal in an airport checkpoint that scans passengers for metallic weapons or devices.
content.spanish: Por favor, coloque todos los objetos metálicos en la bandeja antes de pasar por el detector de metales.
content.usage:
Pattern: metal detector / walk-through scanner
  • Step through the metal detector one at a time.
  • My belt buckle triggered the metal detector.

content.text: We need to clear {{c1::customs control}} before exiting the airport.
content.explanation: To clear 'customs control' means passing through the government checkpoint at an international border to declare goods and verify compliance with imports.
content.spanish: Necesitamos pasar el control de aduanas antes de salir del aeropuerto.
content.usage:
Pattern: customs control / clear customs
  • It took us an hour to clear customs control in London.
  • Do you have anything to declare at customs control?

content.text: Excuse me, where is the office for {{c1::lost and found}}?
content.explanation: The 'lost and found' (or lost property office) is the department in a public facility where lost items are kept for owners to claim.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde está la oficina de objetos perdidos y encontrados?
content.usage:
Pattern: lost and found / lost property
  • I left my umbrella on the plane; I should check the lost and found.
  • The lost and found office is near the baggage claim.

content.text: The captain announced that we are preparing for {{c1::takeoff}}.
content.explanation: 'Takeoff' is the moment or process when an aircraft leaves the ground and rises into the air.
content.spanish: El capitán anunció que nos estamos preparando para el despegue.
content.usage:
Pattern: takeoff / prepare for takeoff
  • Please fasten your seatbelts in preparation for takeoff.
  • The takeoff was very smooth despite the rain.

content.text: Please remain seated with your seatbelt fastened until the plane has finished {{c1::landing}}.
content.explanation: 'Landing' is the process of bringing an aircraft down to the ground or runway at the end of a flight.
content.spanish: Por favor, permanezca sentado con su cinturón abrochado hasta que el avión haya terminado de aterrizar.
content.usage:
Pattern: landing / smooth landing
  • The pilot made a safe landing despite the crosswinds.
  • Remain seated until the landing is complete.

content.text: Is there a currency exchange booth in the {{c1::arrivals hall}}?
content.explanation: The 'arrivals hall' is the public area in an airport terminal where passengers emerge after clearing customs and baggage claim.
content.spanish: ¿Hay una cabina de cambio de divisas en la sala de llegadas?
content.usage:
Pattern: arrivals hall / departures hall
  • My family was waiting for me in the arrivals hall.
  • You can buy a local SIM card in the arrivals hall.

content.text: Passengers are allowed to bring one piece of checked luggage and one {{c1::cabin bag}}.
content.explanation: A 'cabin bag' (or hand luggage) is a small suitcase that complies with airline size rules to be stored in the overhead bins.
content.spanish: Los pasajeros pueden llevar una pieza de equipaje registrado y una maleta de cabina.
content.usage:
Pattern: cabin bag / hand luggage
  • Make sure your cabin bag is not too heavy.
  • I only traveled with a cabin bag to avoid baggage claim.

content.text: If your bags are overweight, you will have to pay an {{c1::excess baggage fee}}.
content.explanation: An 'excess baggage fee' is a charge made by an airline for luggage that exceeds the weight or size allowance.
content.spanish: Si sus maletas tienen sobrepeso, tendrá que pagar una tarifa por exceso de equipaje.
content.usage:
Pattern: excess baggage fee / overweight luggage fee
  • They charged me a $50 excess baggage fee because my suitcase was 5kg over.
  • Avoid the excess baggage fee by wearing your heavy coat.

content.scenario: ✈️ Airport English — Checking Bags: 'I need to check a bag'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
At the airline counter, tell the agent: {{c1::"I have one carry-on and I need to check a bag."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'aɪ hæv wʌn kæ·ri·ɑn ən aɪ niː·də tʃɛ·kə bæg'
Rules applied:
• need to → /niː·də/ (Flap T cross-boundary: /t/ of 'to' is flapped between /d/ and /u/ → reduced to schwa)
• check a → /tʃɛ·kə/ (linking C+V: final /k/ of 'check' links to 'a')

🧠 'Check a bag' means it goes in the cargo hold. 'Carry-on' is the small bag you bring on the plane.
content.spanish: 'Tengo un equipaje de mano y necesito registrar una maleta.' — Registrando equipaje. Fonéticamente, 'need to' se reduce a 'nee-duh' con un Flap T, y 'check a' se convierte en 'che-kuh'.
content.usage:
• "I need to check two bags and I'll keep this backpack as my personal item."
  • "How much does it cost to check a bag?"

content.scenario: ✈️ Airport English — Asking for Gate: 'Where is gate...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To find your departure gate, ask: {{c1::"Excuse me, do you know where gate B12 is?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪk·skjuːz mi, dʒə noʊ wɛr geɪt...'
• do you → /dʒə/ or /jə/ (Yod Coalescence + schwa reduction)
• gate B12 → /ˈgeɪt̚ biː·twɛlv/ (unreleased stop: the /t/ of 'gate' is held and not released before the consonant 'B')

🧠 'Gate' is the specific boarding area. Note the unexploded /t/ at the end of 'gate'.
content.spanish: 'Disculpe, ¿sabe dónde queda la puerta B12?' — Preguntar por la puerta de embarque. Fonética: 'do you' colapsa en 'djuh/juh' y la /t/ en 'gate' queda retenida (sin explosión de aire) antes del sonido de la B.
content.usage:
• "Is this gate B12 for the flight to Chicago?"
  • "My boarding pass says gate C5, is that past security?"

content.scenario: ✈️ Airport English — Flight Crew Requests: 'Can I get a blanket?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
During a flight, to ask the flight attendant for something, say: {{c1::"Can I get a blanket and some water, please?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə blæŋ·kɪ·ɾən sʌm wɑ·ɾər pliːz'
• Can I get a → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə/ (schwa on 'can' + Flap T on 'get a')
• blanket and → /blæŋ·kɪ·ɾən/ (Flap T on 'blanket' before 'and' + H-drop/D-deletion on 'and')

🧠 Politeness rules: 'Can I get...' with a 'please' at the end is the standard friendly request pattern for in-flight service.
content.spanish: '¿Me podría dar una manta y agua, por favor?' — Petición a bordo. Fonéticamente, 'Can I get a' se pronuncia 'kə-naɪ-gɛ-rə' y el final de 'blanket' puede enlazarse como un Flap T suave antes de 'and'.
content.usage:
• "Can I get a set of headphones, please?"
  • "Can I get an extra pillow?"

content.text: Excuse me, could you tell me how to {{c1::get to}} the nearest train station?
content.explanation: get to is the most common conversational phrasal verb meaning to reach or arrive at a destination.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría decirme cómo llegar a la estación de tren más cercana?
content.usage:
Pattern: get to [place]
  • How do I get to the museum from here?
  • It takes 20 minutes to get to the office.

content.text: Go straight ahead for two blocks, then {{c1::take a left}} at the traffic light.
content.explanation: To 'take a left' is a very common idiomatic phrase meaning to turn left at a specific intersection or landmark.
content.spanish: Siga derecho por dos cuadras, luego doble a la izquierda en el semáforo.
content.usage:
Pattern: take a left / make a left / turn left
  • Take a left at the next street.
  • You will need to take a left after you pass the school.

content.text: Excuse me, is there a pharmacy {{c1::within walking distance}} of here?
content.explanation: 'Within walking distance' means close enough to be reached easily by walking (typically less than 10-15 minutes away).
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿hay alguna farmacia a corta distancia a pie de aquí?
content.usage:
Pattern: within walking distance [of something]
  • The hotel is within walking distance of the beach.
  • We live within walking distance of the office.

content.text: You can't miss it; it's right {{c1::across from}} the post office.
content.explanation: 'Across from' means directly opposite or on the other side of a street, hallway, or space.
content.spanish: No tiene pérdida; está justo enfrente de la oficina de correos.
content.usage:
Pattern: across from [place] / opposite
  • The cafe is across from the library.
  • He sat across from me during the meeting.

content.text: To get to the museum, you need to {{c1::cross the street}} and walk past the bank.
content.explanation: To 'cross the street' means to walk from one side of a road to the opposite side.
content.spanish: Para llegar al museo, debe cruzar la calle y pasar el banco.
content.usage:
Pattern: cross the street / cross the road
  • Look both ways before you cross the street.
  • We crossed the street to avoid the construction work.

content.text: Excuse me, could you {{c1::point me in the direction of}} the nearest subway station?
content.explanation: To 'point someone in the direction of' means to show or tell someone the general path or heading to reach a destination.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría indicarme la dirección hacia la estación de metro más cercana?
content.usage:
Pattern: point [someone] in the direction of [place]
  • Can you point me in the direction of the restrooms?
  • She pointed us in the direction of the highway entrance.

content.text: The stadium is located on the {{c1::outer edge}} of the city.
content.explanation: The 'outer edge' refers to the boundary, periphery, or outskirts of an area, such as a city limit.
content.spanish: El estadio está ubicado en las afueras/extremo exterior de la ciudad.
content.usage:
Pattern: outer edge / outskirts
  • They built a new retail park on the outer edge of town.
  • We live on the outer edge of the metropolitan area.

content.text: If you hit the gas station, you've {{c1::gone too far}}; turn back.
content.explanation: To 'go too far' means to travel past the intended destination or target point.
content.spanish: Si llega a la gasolinera, se ha pasado; regrese.
content.usage:
Pattern: go too far / pass the destination
  • If you reach the river, you've gone too far.
  • I think we went too far; we should turn around.

content.text: Take the second exit at the {{c1::roundabout}} to stay on Main Street.
content.explanation: A 'roundabout' (or traffic circle) is a circular intersection where drivers travel counterclockwise (or clockwise in UK) around a central island.
content.spanish: Tome la segunda salida en la rotonda para permanecer en la calle principal.
content.usage:
Pattern: roundabout / traffic circle
  • Go straight through the first roundabout.
  • Turn right at the roundabout.

content.text: Is it quicker to walk or to {{c1::hail a cab}} from here?
content.explanation: To 'hail a cab' (or hail a taxi) means to signal a taxi on the street (typically by raising your arm) to stop and pick you up.
content.spanish: ¿Es más rápido caminar o pedir/hacerle señas a un taxi desde aquí?
content.usage:
Pattern: hail a cab / catch a taxi
  • We stood in the rain trying to hail a cab for fifteen minutes.
  • It is easy to hail a cab in this neighborhood.

content.text: You will find the library right around the {{c1::corner}}.
content.explanation: 'Around the corner' is a very common idiom meaning extremely close or nearby, just on the next street bend.
content.spanish: Encontrará la biblioteca justo a la vuelta de la esquina.
content.usage:
Pattern: around the corner / just past the bend
  • The convenience store is right around the corner.
  • Spring is just around the corner.

content.text: Continue walking until you reach the next {{c1::intersection}}.
content.explanation: An 'intersection' (or junction) is a point where two or more roads cross or meet.
content.spanish: Siga caminando hasta que llegue a la siguiente intersección/cruce.
content.usage:
Pattern: intersection / junction / crossroads
  • Turn right at the next intersection.
  • There was an accident at the busy intersection.

content.text: Excuse me, is this road the quickest way to get to the {{c1::highway}}?
content.explanation: A 'highway' is a main public road, typically one connecting major towns or cities, designed for high-speed traffic.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿esta carretera es la forma más rápida de llegar a la autopista?
content.usage:
Pattern: highway / freeway / interstate
  • Take the exit for the highway.
  • The highway was backed up with traffic.

content.text: The bank is on the left side, just past the {{c1::pedestrian crossing}}.
content.explanation: A 'pedestrian crossing' (or crosswalk) is a specified part of a road where pedestrians have the right of way to cross safely.
content.spanish: El banco está en el lado izquierdo, justo después del paso de peatones.
content.usage:
Pattern: pedestrian crossing / crosswalk / zebra crossing
  • Drivers must stop at the pedestrian crossing.
  • Cross the street only at the pedestrian crossing.

content.text: Excuse me, can you tell me how to get to the nearest {{c1::landmark}}?
content.explanation: A 'landmark' is an object or feature of a landscape or town that is easily seen and recognized from a distance, useful as a reference point.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿podría decirme cómo llegar al punto de referencia más cercano?
content.usage:
Pattern: landmark / monument / focal point
  • The Statue of Liberty is a famous landmark in New York.
  • The clock tower is a key landmark in our city.

content.text: Keep going straight for about half a mile and you'll see a big {{c1::billboard}}.
content.explanation: A 'billboard' (or hoarding) is a large outdoor board for displaying advertisements, commonly located along highways.
content.spanish: Siga recto durante aproximadamente media milla y verá una gran valla publicitaria/espectacular.
content.usage:
Pattern: billboard / advertisement board
  • We advertised our product on a huge billboard near the highway.
  • I noticed the billboard for the new movie.

content.text: If you cross the bridge, you've gone {{c1::in the wrong direction}}.
content.explanation: To go 'in the wrong direction' means heading away from, rather than toward, the target destination.
content.spanish: Si cruzas el puente, has ido en la dirección equivocada.
content.usage:
Pattern: in the wrong direction / off course
  • We walked for two miles in the wrong direction.
  • Turn around; you are going in the wrong direction.

content.text: The museum is just a short {{c1::drive}} from here.
content.explanation: A 'short drive' is a relative measurement of time meaning that a destination is very close when traveling by car (typically under 10-15 minutes).
content.spanish: El museo está a solo un viaje corto en coche desde aquí.
content.usage:
Pattern: short drive / quick ride
  • The airport is just a short drive from our hotel.
  • It is a short drive to the shopping center.

content.text: Is there a shortcut through the park to avoid the {{c1::main street}}?
content.explanation: A 'main street' is the principal street of a town or city, typically containing the primary shops and traffic load.
content.spanish: ¿Hay algún atajo a través del parque para evitar la calle principal?
content.usage:
Pattern: main street / high street
  • The main street was crowded with shoppers.
  • We walked down the main street of the village.

content.text: Pardon me, does this bus {{c1::go towards}} the city center?
content.explanation: To 'go towards' means to travel in the direction of a specific destination or landmark.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿este autobús va hacia el centro de la ciudad?
content.usage:
Pattern: go towards / head in the direction of
  • We walked towards the lake.
  • Does this train go towards the airport?

content.scenario: ☕ Café English — Ordering: 'Can I get a...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish 🔊 Forvo
At a café, the most natural way to order is not 'I would like...' but: {{c1::"Can I get a large oat milk latte, please?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə lɑːrdʒ oʊt mɪlk læ·ɾeɪ pliːz'
Rules applied:
• Can I → /kə·naɪ/ (schwa: 'can' loses its /æ/)
• get a → /gɛ·ɾə/ (Flap T cross-boundary + schwa)
• latte → /ˈlæ·ɾeɪ/ (Flap T within word)

🧠 'I would like' sounds overly formal. 'Can I get' is the default in US/Canadian café culture. 'May I have' sounds like you're in a 1950s movie.
content.spanish: 'Can I get a...' es la forma más natural de pedir en una cafetería en EE.UU. — no 'I would like'. Pronunciación nativa: 'kə-naɪ-gɛ-rə...' — 'can' pierde su vocal /æ/ y se convierte en /kə/ (schwa), y 'get a' se fusiona con un Flap T.
content.usage:
• "Can I get a small black coffee?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə smɔːl blæk ˈkɑ·fi/
  • "Can I get that to go?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·ðæ·tuh goʊ/ (Flap T: 'that to')
  • "Can I get an extra shot?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·ɾæn·ˈɛks·trə ʃɑt/
  • "For here or to go?" — staff question → /fər·hɪr·ər·tuh·goʊ/ (all schwa)

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To customize a café order, common requests are: {{c1::"Can you make that with oat milk?" / "Can I get an extra shot?" / "Can you go light on the ice?"}}
content.explanation:
Key phrases and native pronunciation:
• 'make that with' → /meɪk·ðæ·wɪð/ — linking R/consonant
• 'oat milk' → /ˈoʊt̚·mɪlk/ — unreleased stop: the /t/ of 'oat' is held before 'milk', no burst
• 'light on the' → /laɪ·ɾɑn·ðuh/ — Flap T: 't' of 'light' + 'on' → tap

🧠 'Light on the ice' = less ice. 'Go easy on' = use less of. 'Extra' → /ˈɛks·trə/ (schwa at end).
content.spanish: Personalizar un pedido en la cafetería: 'Can you make that with oat milk?' / 'Can I get an extra shot?' / 'Can you go light on the ice?' — Nota fonética: 'oat milk' tiene una oclusiva no liberada: la /t/ de 'oat' se bloquea antes de 'milk' sin explotar.
content.usage:
• "Can you make that extra hot?" → barista will heat it to a higher temp
  • "Can I get that half sweet / no whip?"
  • "Can you make it a double?" = two espresso shots
  • "Room for cream?" — barista asking if you want space left for dairy
  • "Just a splash of milk." → /splæʃ·əv·mɪlk/ (schwa: 'of' → /əv/)

content.scenario: ☕ Café English — Asking what's in something
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When curious about a menu item, ask: {{c1::"What's in this?" or "What does that come with?"}}
content.explanation:
Native pronunciation:
• 'What's in this?' → /wʌ·sɪn·ðɪs/ — 'What's' + 'in' → linking: the /s/ of 'what's' bridges to 'in'
• 'What does that come with?' → /wʌ·dəz·ðæ·kʌm·wɪð/ — schwa in 'does' /dəz/

🧠 Common follow-up: 'Is there any caffeine in it?' → /ɪz·ðɛr·ɛ·ni·kæ·fiːn·ɪ·nɪt/ — linking 'in it' bridges to a single unit 'ini-nit'.
content.spanish: '¿Qué tiene esto?' → 'What's in this?' /wʌ·sɪn·ðɪs/ — la /s/ de 'What's' y la /ɪ/ de 'in' forman un enlace. '¿Con qué viene eso?' → 'What does that come with?' /wʌ·dəz·ðæ·kʌm·wɪð/ — 'does' se reduce a /dəz/ (schwa).
content.usage:
• "What's in the matcha latte?" (milk + matcha powder + syrup)
  • "Does this have dairy in it?" (checking for allergies)
  • "Is the syrup included?" → /ɪz·ðuh·ˈsɪrəp·ɪŋ·ˈkluːdɪd/
  • "What's the difference between a latte and a flat white?" → common café question
  • Flat white: stronger espresso ratio, microfoam. Latte: more milk, larger.

content.scenario: ☕ Café English — Small talk with the barista
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
The standard barista small talk exchange: Barista: "How are you doing today?" → Best native reply: {{c1::"Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed of 'How are you doing?' → 'haʊ·ər·juh·duː·ɪŋ'
• 'are you' → /ər·juh/ — H-drop + schwa
• 'doing' → /ˈduː·ɪŋ/ (Intrusive glide: /duː-wɪŋ/)

'Pretty good' → /ˈprɪ·ɾi·ɡʊd/ — Flap T: 'pretty' /ˈprɪ·ɾi/
'Yourself?' → short for 'And how about yourself?' — very common ellipsis

🧠 'I'm fine, thank you' sounds formal/robotic. Native speakers say 'Pretty good', 'Not bad', 'Hanging in there', 'Can't complain'.
content.spanish: El intercambio estándar de small talk en una cafetería. 'How are you doing?' → /haʊ·ər·juh·duː·ɪŋ/ (caída de H + schwa). Respuesta más natural: 'Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?' — 'pretty' tiene Flap T: /ˈprɪ·ɾi/. Nunca digas 'I'm fine, thank you' en una cafetería — suena robótico.
content.usage:
• "Pretty good, thanks! Yourself?" ← most natural
  • "Not bad! How about you?" ← slightly more engaged
  • "Can't complain! Busy morning though." ← friendly, opens dialogue
  • "Living the dream!" ← slightly ironic/humorous, works with regulars
  • "Hanging in there." ← honest, slightly tired — barista will empathize

content.scenario: ☕ Café English — When something's wrong with your order
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When your order is wrong, the polite native way is: {{c1::"Hey, sorry to bug you — I think there might be a mistake with my order."}}
content.explanation:
Key phrase breakdown:
• 'sorry to bug you' — 'bug' = bother/disturb (colloquial). Much softer than 'You made a mistake.'
• 'there might be' → /ðər·maɪ·bi/ — schwa: 'there' → /ðər/, 'might' → /maɪ/, 'be' unstressed → /bi/
• 'I think' signals hedging — softens the complaint without backing down

🧠 American complaint culture is indirect: you present it as a possible error, not an accusation. This keeps the interaction positive and gets results faster.
content.spanish: Cuando algo está mal en tu pedido, el estilo nativo americano es indirecto: 'Hey, sorry to bug you — I think there might be a mistake.' No acuses directamente — presenta el problema como un posible error. 'Bug you' = molestarte. 'There might be' → /ðər·maɪ·bi/ (schwa en 'there' y 'be').
content.usage:
• "Excuse me, I ordered a soy latte but this tastes like regular milk."
  • "I think this might be someone else's order."
  • "No worries at all — could you remake this for me?"
  • "My bad — I might not have said it clearly. Could I get it with oat milk?" (own the mistake even if it's theirs — very American)

content.text: A 'drip coffee' is {{c1::regular brewed filter coffee — not espresso based}}. A 'cold brew' is {{c1::coffee steeped in cold water for 12-24 hours — smoother, stronger}}.
content.explanation:
Café vocabulary map:
• Drip/Regular coffee = basic filter brew. 'Can I get a drip?' is common in diners.
• Cold brew vs Iced coffee: cold brew is steeped cold; iced coffee is hot coffee poured over ice.
• Americano = espresso + hot water (similar strength to drip)
• Macchiato → /mæ·kiˈɑː·toʊ/ — Flap T in '-ato': /ˈɑː·ɾoʊ/
• Cortado = espresso + equal parts warm milk (Spanish origin, rising in US)

🧠 At Starbucks: 'a tall' = small, 'a grande' = medium, 'a venti' = large. Yes, it's backwards.
content.spanish: Vocabulario clave de cafetería: Drip coffee = café filtrado normal. Cold brew = café macerado en agua fría 12-24h (más suave y fuerte). Iced coffee = café caliente sobre hielo. Americano = espresso + agua caliente. En Starbucks: tall = pequeño, grande = mediano, venti = grande (sí, está al revés).
content.usage:
• "I'll take a medium drip, room for cream."
  • "What's the difference between cold brew and iced coffee?"
  • "Is the cold brew single or double concentrated?"
  • "I'll go with a cortado — I don't want too much milk."

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When paying at a café, if paying cash say: {{c1::"Keep the change."}} If on a card and tipping feels awkward: {{c1::"I'll tip on the app / I already tipped."}}
content.explanation:
'Keep the change' → /kiːp·ðuh·tʃeɪndʒ/
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa, unstressed)
• Linking: 'keep the' → 'keep-the' fuse — the /p/ of 'keep' attaches to 'the': /kiːp·ðuh/

US tipping culture at cafés: 15-20% is standard at table service. At counter service (Starbucks-style), tipping is optional but the screen will prompt you — 15%, 20%, 25% or custom. Saying 'no tip' is 100% acceptable and not rude at counter service.
content.spanish: 'Keep the change' = quédate con el cambio. En EE.UU., el 15-20% de propina es estándar en servicio de mesa. En mostrador (tipo Starbucks), es opcional — la pantalla te lo pedirá pero puedes decir que no sin ningún problema.
content.usage:
• "Keep the change." (cash, you're being generous)
  • "Can I pay with card?" → 'Cuh-naɪ·peɪ·wɪð·kɑːrd?'
  • "Do you guys take Venmo / Apple Pay?"
  • "Split it — half on each card." (splitting a bill)
  • "Can I get a receipt?" → /kə·naɪ·gɛ·rə·rɪ·siːt/ (Flap T)

content.text: Could I get a medium latte {{c1::to go}}, please?
content.explanation: to go is used when you want to take your food or drink out of the shop instead of consuming it there.
content.spanish: ¿Me da un latte mediano para llevar, por favor?
content.usage:
Pattern: Could I get a [size] [drink] to go, please?
  • Could I get a large Americano to go, please?
  • Could I get a small espresso to go, please?

content.text: I'd like to {{c1::substitute}} whole milk {{c1::with}} oat milk, please.
content.explanation: substitute A with B means to use B instead of A. This is the standard way to customize food or drink orders.
content.spanish: Me gustaría cambiar la leche entera por leche de avena, por favor.
content.usage:
Pattern: substitute [original] with [alternative]
  • Can I substitute white bread with wheat bread?
  • I'd like to substitute fries with a side salad, please.

content.text: Do you have any {{c1::sugar-free}} syrup options?
content.explanation: sugar-free means containing no sugar. Syrups are sweet liquid flavorings added to coffee (like vanilla or caramel).
content.spanish: ¿Tienen opciones de jarabe sin azúcar?
content.usage:
Pattern: [adjective]-free (e.g., gluten-free, caffeine-free, tax-free)
  • Is this cookie gluten-free?
  • I'd prefer decaf, do you have caffeine-free coffee?

content.text: Can I {{c1::pay by card}}?
content.explanation: pay by card means to pay using a credit or debit card rather than cash.
content.spanish: ¿Puedo pagar con tarjeta?
content.usage:
Pattern: pay by [method] (e.g., pay by card, pay by phone, pay by check) but pay in cash
  • You can pay by phone using Apple Pay.
  • Is it cheaper if I pay in cash?

content.text: I would like to order a large cold brew with a {{c1::splash of}} oat milk.
content.explanation: A 'splash of' is an informal measurement meaning a very small amount of liquid (typically milk, cream, or water) added to a drink.
content.spanish: Me gustaría pedir un café extraído en frío grande con un chorrito de leche de avena.
content.usage:
Pattern: splash of [liquid]
  • Just a splash of milk in my tea, please.
  • He likes his whiskey with a splash of soda.

content.text: Do you have any {{c1::dairy-free}} options for the latte?
content.explanation: 'Dairy-free' refers to foods or beverages that contain no milk or milk derivatives, suitable for lactose-intolerant or vegan customers.
content.spanish: ¿Tienen alguna opción sin lácteos para el café con leche?
content.usage:
Pattern: dairy-free milk / plant-based milk
  • Soy and almond milk are common dairy-free alternatives.
  • Is this caramel syrup dairy-free?

content.text: I'll have a double espresso, and can I get that {{c1::sweetened with}} honey?
content.explanation: To get something 'sweetened with' means to add a specific sweetener (like honey, sugar, syrup, or stevia) to a drink or food.
content.spanish: Tomaré un espresso doble, ¿y me lo pueden dar endulzado con miel?
content.usage:
Pattern: sweetened with [sweetener]
  • I prefer my tea sweetened with raw sugar.
  • This lemonade is sweetened with agave nectar.

content.text: Is there a password for the {{c1::guest Wi-Fi}} here?
content.explanation: 'Guest Wi-Fi' is a secondary wireless network provided by businesses for clients and visitors, keeping the internal network secure.
content.spanish: ¿Hay contraseña para el Wi-Fi para invitados aquí?
content.usage:
Pattern: guest Wi-Fi / public network
  • The guest Wi-Fi is free for customers who buy a drink.
  • Ask the barista for the guest Wi-Fi password.

content.text: I'd like to buy a bag of whole bean coffee, could you {{c1::grind it for}} drip?
content.explanation: To 'grind it for [method]' means to crush whole coffee beans into the specific particle size required for a brewing method (e.g. drip, espresso, french press).
content.spanish: Me gustaría comprar una bolsa de café en grano entero, ¿podrían molerlo para cafetera de filtro?
content.usage:
Pattern: grind [coffee] for [brewing method]
  • Could you grind these beans for a French press?
  • I grind my own coffee beans for espresso every morning.

content.text: Can I get a pastry to go, and please {{c1::warm it up}} for me?
content.explanation: To 'warm up' something means to make it warm or hot, usually by putting it in a microwave, oven, or toaster.
content.spanish: ¿Me da una factura/pan dulce para llevar, y por favor me la calienta?
content.usage:
Pattern: warm [something] up / heat up
  • Would you like me to warm up your croissant?
  • I need to warm up my lunch in the microwave.

content.text: I'll have a decaf latte, and please make sure it is {{c1::extra hot}}.
content.explanation: 'Extra hot' is a specific order request asking the barista to steam the milk to a higher temperature (typically around 160-170°F).
content.spanish: Tomaré un café con leche descafeinado, y por favor asegúrese de que esté extra caliente.
content.usage:
Pattern: extra hot / steam the milk extra hot
  • Could you make my mocha extra hot?
  • I like my cappuccino extra hot when it is freezing outside.

content.text: Do you have any sugar substitutes like stevia or {{c1::artificial sweeteners}}?
content.explanation: An 'artificial sweetener' is a food additive that provides a sweet taste like that of sugar while containing significantly less food energy.
content.spanish: ¿Tienen algún sustituto de azúcar como stevia o edulcorantes artificiales?
content.usage:
Pattern: artificial sweeteners / sugar substitutes
  • I don't use sugar; I prefer artificial sweeteners.
  • Does this coffee shop have any artificial sweeteners like Splenda?

content.text: I'd like to sit outside; is there any table available on the {{c1::patio}}?
content.explanation: A 'patio' (or terrace) is an outdoor space adjacent to a cafe or house, typically paved and used for dining or recreation.
content.spanish: Me gustaría sentarme afuera; ¿hay alguna mesa disponible en la terraza/patio?
content.usage:
Pattern: patio / outdoor seating / terrace
  • Let's sit on the patio to enjoy the sunny weather.
  • The patio was packed with customers.

content.text: Can I get a slice of banana bread and please {{c1::toast it}} for me?
content.explanation: To 'toast' a pastry means to heat it using a toaster or grill until it is warm and slightly crispy on the outside.
content.spanish: ¿Me da una rebanada de pan de plátano y por favor me la tuesta?
content.usage:
Pattern: toast it / toasted bread
  • Would you like me to toast the bagel for you?
  • I prefer my bread lightly toasted.

content.text: I'll buy this mug; does it come with a lid or is it sold {{c1::as is}}?
content.explanation: 'As is' is a legal and commercial phrase meaning that the product is sold in its current condition, with all faults, without any warranty.
content.spanish: Compraré esta taza; ¿viene con tapa o se vende tal como está?
content.usage:
Pattern: sold as is / buy as is
  • The defective device was sold as is with no returns.
  • We bought the display model as is for a discount.

content.text: Excuse me, where can I find some napkins and {{c1::stirrers}}?
content.explanation: A 'stirrer' is a small wooden or plastic stick used to mix sugar or milk into a hot beverage like coffee or tea.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde puedo encontrar algunas servilletas y agitadores?
content.usage:
Pattern: wooden stirrers / plastic stirrers
  • Wooden stirrers are more eco-friendly than plastic ones.
  • Please grab a stirrer from the condiment counter.

content.text: I'd like to purchase some coffee beans; which blend do you {{c1::recommend for}} espresso?
content.explanation: To 'recommend for [method]' is the standard collocation used when asking a barista or merchant which product matches a specific preparation method.
content.spanish: Me gustaría comprar algunos granos de café; ¿qué mezcla me recomienda para espresso?
content.usage:
Pattern: recommend for [brewing method / use case]
  • Which coffee beans do you recommend for cold brew?
  • The barista recommended this dark roast for espresso.

content.text: Could you add some cocoa powder or cinnamon on {{c1::top of}} the foam?
content.explanation: 'On top of' is a prepositional phrase meaning directly on the upper surface of something.
content.spanish: ¿Podría agregar un poco de cacao en polvo o canela encima de la espuma?
content.usage:
Pattern: on top of [surface / foam]
  • Sprinkle some chocolate flakes on top of the cream.
  • He put his keys on top of the table.

content.text: Do you offer free refills on drip coffee if I {{c1::dine in}}?
content.explanation: To 'dine in' (or eat in) means to consume your food or drink inside the restaurant or cafe premises, as opposed to taking it 'to go'.
content.spanish: ¿Ofrecen recargas gratuitas de café de filtro si consumo aquí?
content.usage:
Pattern: dine in / eat in / to go
  • Would you like to dine in or is this to go?
  • Dine-in customers get free water refills.

content.text: I'll pay with my phone using the contactless {{c1::payment reader}}.
content.explanation: A 'payment reader' (or card terminal) is the electronic device that scans credit cards or mobile wallets using NFC for transactions.
content.spanish: Pagaré con mi teléfono usando el lector de pagos sin contacto.
content.usage:
Pattern: payment reader / card reader / card terminal
  • Hold your phone near the payment reader to complete the transaction.
  • The payment reader is not responding.

content.text: Can I get a {{c1::coffee}} to go, please?
content.explanation: In everyday speech, native speakers often say 'Can I get...' instead of 'Could I have...' for a casual request. The phrase is common in cafés and shops.
content.spanish: ¿Podría pedir un café para llevar, por favor?
content.usage:
Pattern: Can I get ...?
  • Can I get a latte?
  • Can I get a table for two?

content.text: What would you {{c1::recommend}} from the menu today?
content.explanation: Recommend is a common polite phrase when asking for a suggestion from a waiter, barista, or host.
content.spanish: ¿Qué recomendaría usted del menú hoy?
content.usage:
Pattern: What would you recommend?
  • What would you recommend for lunch?
  • What do you recommend for someone who is vegetarian?

content.scenario: Colloquial English: Special of the Day ⭐
content.text: What’s the {{c1::special of the day}}?
content.explanation: The special of the day is a restaurant or café promotion or featured dish that the staff recommends.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el especial del día?
content.usage:
Pattern: special of the day / house special
  • What’s the special of the day?
  • The house special is the grilled chicken sandwich.

content.text: Could you make it {{c1::extra hot}}?
content.explanation: In cafés, extra hot means that the drink should be heated more than usual. This phrase is very common in casual service interactions.
content.spanish: ¿Podría hacerlo extra caliente?
content.usage:
Pattern: extra hot / extra milk / no sugar
  • Could you make it extra hot?
  • Can I get it with oat milk?

content.text: Is this {{c1::gluten-free}}?
content.explanation: Gluten-free is a common diet-related phrase when asking about food ingredients or allergies.
content.spanish: ¿Esto es sin gluten?
content.usage:
Pattern: Is this gluten-free?
  • Is this dish gluten-free?
  • Do you have anything without dairy?

content.text: Can we {{c1::split the bill}}?
content.explanation: To split the bill means to divide the total cost between people, especially in restaurants or cafés.
content.spanish: ¿Podemos dividir la cuenta?
content.usage:
Pattern: split the bill / separate checks
  • Can we split the bill?
  • Could we get separate checks?

content.scenario: Colloquial English: That Hit the Spot 😋
content.text: That really {{c1::hit the spot}}.
content.explanation: This idiom means the food or drink was exactly what you needed and tasted great.
content.spanish: Eso sí que me satisfizo mucho.
content.usage:
Pattern: hit the spot
  • That coffee really hit the spot.
  • The soup hit the spot on a cold day.

content.scenario: Colloquial English: Cut Back on Sugar 🍰
content.text: I’m trying to {{c1::cut back on}} sugar these days.
content.explanation: To cut back on something means to reduce the amount of it. It is a very natural phrase in casual conversation.
content.spanish: Estoy tratando de reducir el azúcar estos días.
content.usage:
Pattern: cut back on
  • I’m trying to cut back on caffeine.
  • We are cutting back on spending.

content.text: Would you like to {{c1::grab a drink}} sometime?
content.explanation: grab a drink is a casual, friendly way to invite someone to go out for a beverage (coffee, beer, or cocktails).
content.spanish: ¿Te gustaría ir a tomar algo alguna vez?
content.usage:
Pattern: grab a [item] (e.g. grab a coffee, grab a bite to eat, grab lunch)
  • Let's grab a coffee this afternoon.
  • Do you want to grab a bite to eat after work?

content.text: I had a great time tonight, we should {{c1::do this again}}.
content.explanation: do this again is a friendly, low-pressure phrase used at the end of a good social gathering or date to show interest in hanging out again.
content.spanish: La pasé genial esta noche, deberíamos repetir esto.
content.usage:
Pattern: we should do this again [sometime/soon]
  • I really enjoyed the museum. We should do this again sometime!
  • It was great catching up. We should do this again soon.

content.text: Would you like to go out for a cup of coffee sometime next week, or perhaps grab a {{c1::bite to eat}}?
content.explanation: To 'grab a bite to eat' is a highly common, informal idiom meaning to get a quick meal or snack together.
content.spanish: ¿Te gustaría salir a tomar una taza de café en algún momento de la próxima semana, o tal vez comer algo rápido?
content.usage:
Pattern: grab a bite to eat / get lunch
  • Let's grab a bite to eat before the movie starts.
  • I only have fifteen minutes to grab a bite to eat.

content.text: I had a wonderful time tonight; thank you for showing me your favorite spot in the {{c1::city}}.
content.explanation: A 'favorite spot' refers to a person's preferred place (like a cafe, park view, or hidden bar) for leisure or dining.
content.spanish: La pasé de maravilla esta noche; gracias por mostrarme tu lugar favorito en la ciudad.
content.usage:
Pattern: favorite spot / local hangout
  • This coffee shop is my favorite spot for reading.
  • She showed me her favorite spot in the botanical gardens.

content.text: Let me know when you are free; I'd love to take you out to that new Italian restaurant we talked {{c1::about}}.
content.explanation: To 'talk about' something is the phrasal verb meaning to discuss a topic or place in conversation.
content.spanish: Avísame cuando estés libre; me encantaría llevarte a ese nuevo restaurante italiano del que hablamos.
content.usage:
Pattern: talk about [place / topic]
  • This is the book I was talking about yesterday.
  • We talked about the database design for hours.

content.text: I really enjoy talking to you; we seem to have a lot of common interests and share a similar {{c1::sense of humor}}.
content.explanation: A 'sense of humor' is a person's capability to perceive, appreciate, or express what is funny or amusing.
content.spanish: Realmente disfruto hablar contigo; parece que tenemos muchos intereses en común y compartimos un sentido del humor similar.
content.usage:
Pattern: sense of humor / witty / dry humor
  • He has a dry sense of humor that is hard to understand at first.
  • A shared sense of humor is key to a good relationship.

content.text: Are you free this weekend, or do you already have plans with your {{c1::friends}}?
content.explanation: To 'have plans' means to have pre-arranged activities or commitments with friends or family.
content.spanish: ¿Estás libre este fin de semana, o ya tienes planes con tus amigos?
content.usage:
Pattern: have plans / busy this weekend
  • I'd love to come, but I already have plans.
  • Do you have plans for the holidays?

content.text: I'd love to see you again; let's keep in touch and schedule something for next {{c1::weekend}}.
content.explanation: To 'keep in touch' is an idiom meaning to maintain communication or contact with someone over time.
content.spanish: Me encantaría verte de nuevo; mantengámonos en contacto y programemos algo para el próximo fin de semana.
content.usage:
Pattern: keep in touch / stay in contact
  • We promised to keep in touch after graduation.
  • Let's keep in touch via email.

content.text: Thank you for the dinner; it was very kind of you to treat me, and I'd like to pay next {{c1::time}}.
content.explanation: To 'pay next time' is a polite dating convention indicating that the recipient of a free meal wants to reciprocate by paying for the next date.
content.spanish: Gracias por la cena; fue muy amable de tu parte invitarme, y me gustaría pagar la próxima vez.
content.usage:
Pattern: pay next time / my treat next time
  • You paid today, so let me pay next time.
  • She insisted on paying next time.

content.text: Let's go for a walk in the park; it's a beautiful day and a great opportunity to chat and get to {{c1::know each other better}}.
content.explanation: To 'get to know each other better' means to learn more about a person's life, opinions, and character through conversation and shared experiences.
content.spanish: Vamos a dar un paseo por el parque; es un hermoso día y una gran oportunidad para charlar y conocernos mejor.
content.usage:
Pattern: get to know each other better
  • Dating is a process of getting to know each other better.
  • I want to get to know my teammates better.

content.text: Would you like to grab a drink after work this Friday? I know a great little place near the {{c1::office}}.
content.explanation: The 'office' is the physical workplace where colleagues work, commonly used as a reference point for post-work socializing.
content.spanish: ¿Te gustaría ir a tomar un trago después del trabajo este viernes? Conozco un gran lugarcito cerca de la oficina.
content.usage:
Pattern: near the office / head to the office
  • Let's meet at the pub near the office.
  • I am working from the office today.

content.text: I had a wonderful time chatting with you tonight; thank you for the lovely conversation and the {{c1::company}}.
content.explanation: 'Company' in this social context refers to the presence of another person or group, here appreciated at the end of a date.
content.spanish: Me divertí mucho charlando contigo esta noche; gracias por la agradable conversación y la compañía.
content.usage:
Pattern: enjoy the company / keep someone company
  • Thank you for keeping me company while I waited for the train.
  • I really enjoy your company.

content.text: Let me know when you are free next week; I'd love to take you to that new art gallery that just {{c1::opened}}.
content.explanation: To 'open' (or be newly opened) is the verb describing a new business, exhibition, or venue welcoming the public for the first time.
content.spanish: Avísame cuando estés libre la próxima semana; me encantaría llevarte a esa nueva galería de arte que acaba de abrir.
content.usage:
Pattern: just opened / newly opened
  • A new coffee shop just opened around the corner.
  • The museum opened its doors to the public yesterday.

content.text: I really enjoy spending time with you; you are very easy to talk to and have a great {{c1::personality}}.
content.explanation: A 'personality' is the combination of characteristics, qualities, and behaviors that form an individual's distinctive character.
content.spanish: Realmente disfruto pasar tiempo contigo; es muy fácil hablar contigo y tienes una gran personalidad.
content.usage:
Pattern: great personality / outgoing personality
  • She has a very warm and outgoing personality.
  • I like him because of his great personality.

content.text: Are you busy this weekend, or do you have some free time to hang out and grab a {{c1::bite to eat}}?
content.explanation: To 'grab a bite to eat' is a common informal idiom meaning to get a quick meal together, used here to invite someone on a casual date.
content.spanish: ¿Estás ocupado este fin de semana, o tienes algo de tiempo libre para pasar el rato y comer algo rápido?
content.usage:
Pattern: grab a bite to eat / get food
  • Let's grab a bite to eat after the movie.
  • I need to grab a quick bite to eat.

content.text: I'd love to see you again soon; let's keep in touch and plan something for next {{c1::Saturday}}.
content.explanation: 'Saturday' is the first day of the weekend, the most common day for arranging social dates or events.
content.spanish: Me encantaría verte de nuevo pronto; mantengámonos en contacto y planifiquemos algo para el próximo sábado.
content.usage:
Pattern: next Saturday / Saturday night
  • Are you free next Saturday afternoon?
  • We went to a concert last Saturday.

content.text: Thank you for the coffee; it was very nice of you to pay, and I'll treat you next {{c1::time}}.
content.explanation: To 'treat' someone next 'time' means to pay for their drink, meal, or ticket on the subsequent occasion as a reciprocal gesture.
content.spanish: Gracias por el café; fue muy amable de tu parte pagar, y yo te invito la próxima vez.
content.usage:
Pattern: treat you next time / my treat next time
  • Put your wallet away; it's my treat today, and you can treat me next time.
  • She treated us to dinner yesterday.

content.text: Let's go for a walk by the river; it's a beautiful evening and a nice opportunity to chat and get to {{c1::know each other better}}.
content.explanation: To 'get to know each other better' is the standard phrase for learning more about a romantic partner's background, tastes, and values.
content.spanish: Vamos a dar un paseo junto al río; es una hermosa tarde y una buena oportunidad para charlar y conocernos mejor.
content.usage:
Pattern: get to know each other better / build a connection
  • We spent the evening talking and getting to know each other better.
  • Our goal is to get to know our clients better.

content.text: I'll text you when I get home to let you know I arrived safely; thank you for a wonderful {{c1::date}}.
content.explanation: A 'date' is a pre-arranged social or romantic meeting, especially between two people who are interested in each other.
content.spanish: Te enviaré un mensaje de texto cuando llegue a casa para avisarte que llegué bien; gracias por una cita maravillosa.
content.usage:
Pattern: first date / go on a date
  • Our first date was at a small sushi restaurant.
  • He asked her out on a date.

content.text: Would you like to join me for a concert next month? I have an extra ticket and would love for you to {{c1::come along}}.
content.explanation: To 'come along' is a phrasal verb meaning to accompany someone, go with them, or join them on a trip or event.
content.spanish: ¿Te gustaría acompañarme a un concierto el próximo mes? Tengo una entrada extra y me encantaría que me acompañaras.
content.usage:
Pattern: come along / join in
  • We are going to the beach; do you want to come along?
  • She invited me to come along to the gallery.

content.text: The weather is so {{c1::unpredictable}} lately.
content.explanation: Unpredictable is a natural adjective for discussing weather and changing conditions in social conversation.
content.spanish: El clima ha sido tan impredecible últimamente.
content.usage:
Pattern: the weather is ...
  • The weather is amazing today.
  • The weather has been awful all week.

content.scenario: Everyday English: What Did You Do Yesterday? 🗓️
content.text: I just {{c1::hung out}} with some friends yesterday.
content.explanation: To hang out means to spend time casually with people, often in a relaxed or informal way.
content.spanish: Ayer simplemente pasé el rato con unos amigos.
content.usage:
Pattern: hang out with
  • I hung out at the park.
  • We hung out after work.

content.text: I’m really into {{c1::photography}} and hiking.
content.explanation: To be into something means to like it or be interested in it a lot.
content.spanish: Me interesa mucho la fotografía y el senderismo.
content.usage:
Pattern: I’m into ...
  • I’m into cooking.
  • She’s really into music.

content.text: I try not to talk too much about {{c1::politics}} at work.
content.explanation: Politics is an everyday topic, but people often use it carefully in social or professional contexts.
content.spanish: Intento no hablar demasiado de política en el trabajo.
content.usage:
Pattern: talk about politics / political issues
  • Politics is a sensitive topic.
  • We discussed political issues at dinner.

content.text: We’re keeping the weekend {{c1::pretty chill}}.
content.explanation: Pretty chill means relaxed, low-stress, and not too busy. This is very natural in conversational English.
content.spanish: Vamos a tener un fin de semana bastante relajado.
content.usage:
Pattern: pretty chill / keep it simple
  • We’re keeping it pretty chill.
  • I just want a chill weekend.

content.text: I caught the {{c1::news}} this morning before work.
content.explanation: To catch the news means to watch or hear the news briefly. It is a natural phrase in daily conversation.
content.spanish: Vi las noticias esta mañana antes de ir al trabajo.
content.usage:
Pattern: catch the news / hear the news
  • I caught the news on the radio.
  • Did you hear the news?

content.text: It’s been a pretty {{c1::chill}} week so far.
content.explanation: Chill is a casual adjective meaning relaxed or calm. It is used a lot in informal speech.
content.spanish: Ha sido una semana bastante tranquila hasta ahora.
content.usage:
Pattern: pretty chill / a chill day
  • It’s a chill evening.
  • We had a chill lunch together.

content.text: I’m not really sure {{c1::what to think}} about it.
content.explanation: This phrase is very common in casual conversation when someone is uncertain or ambivalent.
content.spanish: No estoy realmente seguro de qué pensar al respecto.
content.usage:
Pattern: not sure what to think
  • I’m not sure what to think about the new policy.
  • She wasn’t sure what to say.

content.text: Looking back: "I {{c1::vividly remember}} visiting the Andes; it was an {{c2::eye-opening experience}} that changed how I saw nature."
content.explanation: Vividly remember means to recall with clear, bright, and detailed memory. **Eye-opening experience** is an event that teaches you something surprising or changes your perspective.
content.spanish: Mirando hacia atrás: "Recuerdo vívidamente haber visitado los Andes; fue una experiencia reveladora que cambió mi forma de ver la naturaleza".
content.usage:
Pattern: vividly remember [action/event] / eye-opening experience
  • I vividly remember my first coding project.
  • Working in tech support was an eye-opening experience.

content.text: Recalling his last job: "I was {{c1::snowed under}} with tickets, and when the server crashed, I was at my {{c2::wit's end}}."
content.explanation: Snowed under is an idiom meaning overwhelmed with work. **At one's wit's end** is an idiom meaning completely stressed out and not knowing what to do next.
content.spanish: Recordando su último trabajo: "Estaba inundado de tickets, y cuando el servidor se cayó, estaba al borde de la locura (no sabía qué hacer)".
content.usage:
Pattern: be snowed under with [work] / at my wit's end
  • I'm completely snowed under with homework this semester.
  • I was at my wit's end trying to solve the connection bug.

content.text: Telling the story: "We crossed paths {{c1::out of the blue}} in Berlin, and we {{c2::hit it off}} immediately."
content.explanation: Out of the blue is an idiom meaning completely unexpected. **Hit it off** is a phrasal verb meaning to quickly become friendly or connect with someone.
content.spanish: Contando la historia: "Nos cruzamos por casualidad (de la nada) en Berlín, y congeniamos de inmediato".
content.usage:
Pattern: cross paths out of the blue / hit it off with [someone]
  • I ran into my former colleague out of the blue.
  • We hit it off during our first meeting at the conference.

content.text: Relating the emergency: "I got a {{c1::minor gash}} on my arm, but the doctor bandaged it and issued a {{c2::medical discharge}} within an hour."
content.explanation: Gash is a long, deep cut or wound in the skin. **Medical discharge** is the official release of a patient from medical care.
content.spanish: Relatando la emergencia: "Me hice un corte menor en el brazo, pero el médico lo vendó y emitió un alta médica en una hora".
content.usage:
Pattern: gash on [body part] / receive a medical discharge
  • He had a deep gash on his knee after falling.
  • The hospital prepared his medical discharge papers.

content.text: Reflecting on the project: "In {{c1::hindsight}}, the failed launch was a blessing in disguise because our {{c2::key takeaway}} was the need for automated tests."
content.explanation: In hindsight means looking back at an event in the past. **Blessing in disguise** is an idiom meaning something bad that turns out to have good results. **Takeaway** is the key lesson learned.
content.spanish: Reflexionando sobre el proyecto: "En retrospectiva, el lanzamiento fallido fue una bendición disfrazada porque nuestro principal aprendizaje fue la necesidad de pruebas automatizadas".
content.usage:
Pattern: in hindsight, [situation] was a blessing in disguise / key takeaway from [event]
  • In hindsight, losing that client helped us focus on better partners.
  • The key takeaway from the security audit was to rotate keys immediately.

content.text: To make a long story short, we ended up missing our flight and had to spend the night at the {{c1::airport terminal}}.
content.explanation: To 'make a long story short' is a very common idiom used to skip minor details and summarize the final outcome of an event quickly.
content.spanish: Para no hacerles el cuento largo (resumiendo), terminamos perdiendo nuestro vuelo y tuvimos que pasar la noche en la terminal del aeropuerto.
content.usage:
Pattern: to make a long story short / long story short
  • Long story short, I got the job.
  • To make a long story short, we had to walk home in the rain.

content.text: Believe it or not, she actually won the lottery but decided to donate all the winnings to {{c1::charity}}.
content.explanation: 'Believe it or not' is a conversational expression used to introduce a surprising, remarkable, or unexpected fact that is nevertheless true.
content.spanish: Lo creas o no, ella realmente ganó la lotería pero decidió donar todas las ganancias a la caridad.
content.usage:
Pattern: believe it or not / surprising fact
  • Believe it or not, I've never eaten sushi.
  • He completed the marathon in two hours, believe it or not.

content.text: Out of the blue, my childhood friend called me after ten years of no contact, which was a pleasant {{c1::surprise}}.
content.explanation: 'Out of the blue' is an idiom meaning completely unexpectedly, suddenly, or without any warning.
content.spanish: De la nada (inesperadamente), mi amigo de la infancia me llamó después de diez años sin contacto, lo cual fue una grata sorpresa.
content.usage:
Pattern: out of the blue / suddenly
  • She quit her job out of the blue.
  • He showed up at my house out of the blue.

content.text: To cut to the chase, we need to approve the proposal today if we want to meet our launch {{c1::deadline}}.
content.explanation: To 'cut to the chase' is an idiom meaning to stop talking about minor details and address the most important part of a matter directly.
content.spanish: Para ir al grano (resumiendo), necesitamos aprobar la propuesta hoy si queremos cumplir con nuestra fecha límite de lanzamiento.
content.usage:
Pattern: cut to the chase / get straight to point
  • Let's cut to the chase: how much will this cost?
  • The director cut to the chase and announced the layoffs.

content.text: As luck would have it, I found my lost keys right when I was about to call a locksmith to {{c1::replace them}}.
content.explanation: 'As luck would have it' is an idiom meaning by chance or fortune (can be used for both good and bad luck).
content.spanish: Como quiso la suerte (por suerte), encontré mis llaves perdidas justo cuando estaba a punto de llamar a un cerrajero para reemplazarlas.
content.usage:
Pattern: as luck would have it / fortunately / unfortunately
  • As luck would have it, our train was delayed by two hours.
  • I forgot my umbrella, but as luck would have it, it didn't rain.

content.text: To make a long story short, we missed the train and had to wait at the station for {{c1::three hours}}.
content.explanation: 'Three hours' is a specific duration of time, used here to complete the storytelling outcome of missing a train.
content.spanish: Para abreviar la historia (resumiendo), perdimos el tren y tuvimos que esperar en la estación durante tres horas.
content.usage:
Pattern: wait for three hours / three hours remaining
  • The database backup took three hours to complete.
  • We stood in line for three hours.

content.text: Believe it or not, he actually won the competition despite having never practiced the sport {{c1::before}}.
content.explanation: 'Before' is an adverb meaning at a previous time; in the past, completing the contrast of winning without prior experience.
content.spanish: Lo creas o no, él realmente ganó la competencia a pesar de no haber practicado nunca ese deporte antes.
content.usage:
Pattern: never before / day before / before yesterday
  • I have never seen this error message before.
  • Read the instructions before starting the setup.

content.text: Out of the blue, my former manager emailed me with a job offer, which was a pleasant {{c1::surprise}}.
content.explanation: A 'surprise' is an unexpected or astonishing event, fact, or thing, here described as a positive outcome.
content.spanish: De la nada (inesperadamente), mi antiguo gerente me envió un correo electrónico con una oferta de trabajo, lo cual fue una grata sorpresa.
content.usage:
Pattern: pleasant surprise / complete surprise
  • His sudden promotion was a complete surprise.
  • The nice weather was a pleasant surprise.

content.text: To cut to the chase, we need to approve the budget today if we want to meet our launch {{c1::deadline}}.
content.explanation: A 'deadline' is the latest time or date by which something must be completed or submitted.
content.spanish: Para ir al grano (resumiendo), necesitamos aprobar el presupuesto hoy si queremos cumplir con nuestra fecha límite de lanzamiento.
content.usage:
Pattern: meet the deadline / miss the deadline / strict deadline
  • We must work overtime to meet the launch deadline.
  • Missing the deadline will delay the product release.

content.text: As luck would have it, I found my passport right when I was about to call the embassy to {{c1::replace it}}.
content.explanation: To 'replace' something means to provide a substitute or equivalent in place of an item that was lost, damaged, or expired.
content.spanish: Como quiso la suerte (por suerte), encontré mi pasaporte justo cuando estaba a punto de llamar a la embajada para reemplazarlo.
content.usage:
Pattern: replace it / replace device / replacement parts
  • I lost my credit card and had to call the bank to replace it.
  • We need to replace the old database servers.

content.text: You won't believe what happened next: the car ran out of gas right in the middle of the {{c1::bridge}}.
content.explanation: A 'bridge' is a structure carrying a road, path, or railway across a river, valley, or road obstacle.
content.spanish: No vas a creer lo que pasó después: el auto se quedó sin gasolina justo en medio del puente.
content.usage:
Pattern: cross the bridge / suspension bridge
  • The car broke down right on the bridge.
  • We crossed the bridge to enter the city center.

content.text: Long story short, we had to move the wedding indoors due to the sudden heavy rain and strong {{c1::winds}}.
content.explanation: 'Wind' is the perceptible natural movement of the air, especially in the form of a current of air blowing through an area.
content.spanish: En resumen, tuvimos que trasladar la boda adentro (bajo techo) debido a la repentina lluvia intensa y los fuertes vientos.
content.usage:
Pattern: strong winds / heavy winds / gust of wind
  • The trees were blown down by strong winds.
  • Strong winds delayed the flight takeoff.

content.text: All of a sudden, the power went out, leaving the entire neighborhood in complete {{c1::darkness}}.
content.explanation: 'All of a sudden' is an idiom meaning completely unexpectedly, suddenly, or without warning.
content.spanish: De repente (de la nada), la energía se cortó, dejando a todo el vecindario en completa oscuridad.
content.usage:
Pattern: all of a sudden / suddenly / out of the blue
  • All of a sudden, the database stopped responding.
  • She stood up and left all of a sudden.

content.text: To make matters worse, it started to rain heavily right when we got a flat tire on the {{c1::highway}}.
content.explanation: 'To make matters worse' is an idiom meaning to make a bad situation even more difficult, complicated, or unpleasant.
content.spanish: Para colmo de males (para empeorar las cosas), comenzó a llover fuertemente justo cuando tuvimos una llanta ponchada en la autopista.
content.usage:
Pattern: to make matters worse / exacerbate issue
  • I forgot my keys, and to make matters worse, it started to pour.
  • The server crashed, and to make matters worse, the backup was corrupted.

content.text: At the end of the day, what matters most is that everyone arrived home safely and {{c1::unharmed}}.
content.explanation: 'At the end of the day' is a very common idiom meaning ultimately, when everything is taken into consideration.
content.spanish: Al fin y al cabo (al final del día), lo que más importa es que todos llegaron a casa seguros e ilesos.
content.usage:
Pattern: at the end of the day / ultimately
  • At the end of the day, we must deliver value to the client.
  • It is a tough decision, but at the end of the day, it is your choice.

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Formal greeting used until noon'? {{c1::Good morning}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Formal greeting used until noon' en English? Good morning
content.usage: Term: Good morning | Meaning: Formal greeting used until noon

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Formal greeting used from noon until 6 PM'? {{c1::Good afternoon}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Formal greeting used from noon until 6 PM' en English? Good afternoon
content.usage: Term: Good afternoon | Meaning: Formal greeting used from noon until 6 PM

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Formal greeting used after 6 PM'? {{c1::Good evening}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Formal greeting used after 6 PM' en English? Good evening
content.usage: Term: Good evening | Meaning: Formal greeting used after 6 PM

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Informal greeting meaning "How are you?"'? {{c1::What's up?}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Informal greeting meaning "How are you?"' en English? What's up?
content.usage: Term: What's up? | Meaning: Informal greeting meaning "How are you?"

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Standard way to say farewell'? {{c1::Goodbye}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Standard way to say farewell' en English? Goodbye
content.usage: Term: Goodbye | Meaning: Standard way to say farewell

content.text: In English, how do you say 'Farewell used when leaving late or going to bed'? {{c1::Good night}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Greetings and Farewells'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Farewell used when leaving late or going to bed' en English? Good night
content.usage: Term: Good night | Meaning: Farewell used when leaving late or going to bed

content.text: Hello, my name is Jesus. What is {{c1::your name}}?
content.explanation: 'What is your name?' is the standard fundamental question used to ask someone for their name during a first introduction.
content.spanish: Hola, mi nombre es Jesús. ¿Cómo te llamas? (¿Cuál es tu nombre?)
content.usage:
Pattern: my name is [Name] / what is your name
  • Hello, my name is Colin. What is your name?
  • Her name is Maria.

content.text: Nice to {{c1::meet you}}, Colin. How are you doing today?
content.explanation: 'Nice to meet you' is the polite greeting phrase used when meeting someone for the first time.
content.spanish: Gusto en conocerte, Colin. ¿Cómo estás hoy?
content.usage:
Pattern: nice to meet you / pleased to meet you
  • Nice to meet you, too.
  • It is a pleasure to meet you.

content.text: I'm doing well, thank you. And {{c1::how about you}}?
content.explanation: 'How about you?' (or 'and you?') is a reciprocal question used to ask the speaker the same question they just asked you.
content.spanish: Me va bien, gracias. ¿Y qué tal tú? (¿Y tú?)
content.usage:
Pattern: how about you / and you
  • I'm tired, how about you?
  • I like coffee, how about you?

content.text: Good morning! Did you {{c1::sleep well}} last night?
content.explanation: To 'sleep well' is a standard friendly check used in the morning to ask someone if they rested comfortably during the night.
content.spanish: ¡Buenos días! ¿Dormiste bien anoche?
content.usage:
Pattern: sleep well / rest well
  • Did you sleep well? Yes, I slept like a baby.
  • Goodnight, sleep well!

content.text: Goodbye! Have a {{c1::nice day}} and see you later!
content.explanation: 'Have a nice day' is a polite way to say goodbye to someone during the daytime.
content.spanish: ¡Adiós! Que tengas un buen día y ¡nos vemos luego!
content.usage:
Pattern: have a nice day / have a good day
  • Thank you, have a nice day!
  • The cashier told me to have a nice day.

content.text: It was a pleasure meeting you; hope to see you again {{c1::soon}}!
content.explanation: 'Soon' is an adverb meaning in a short time from now; not long after.
content.spanish: Fue un placer conocerte; ¡espero verte de nuevo pronto!
content.usage:
Pattern: see you soon / see you later
  • Goodbye, see you soon!
  • The train will arrive soon.

content.text: Good evening! Welcome to our restaurant; do you have a {{c1::reservation}}?
content.explanation: A 'reservation' is an arrangement to secure a table in a restaurant or room in a hotel in advance.
content.spanish: ¡Buenas noches! Bienvenidos a nuestro restaurante; ¿tienen una reservación?
content.usage:
Pattern: have a reservation / book a table
  • I have a reservation under Smith.
  • I'd like to book a table for two.

content.text: Have a safe trip home, and please text me when you {{c1::arrive}}!
content.explanation: To 'arrive' means to reach a destination at the end of a journey.
content.spanish: ¡Que tengas un viaje seguro a casa, y por favor envíame un mensaje cuando llegues!
content.usage:
Pattern: arrive home / arrive at airport
  • Text me when you arrive home safely.
  • We arrived at the hotel at midnight.

content.text: See you tomorrow morning at the office! Have a {{c1::good night}}!
content.explanation: 'Good night' is a common farewell spoken when parting from someone at night or before going to sleep.
content.spanish: ¡Nos vemos mañana por la mañana en la oficina! ¡Buenas noches!
content.usage:
Pattern: good night / goodnight
  • Good night, see you tomorrow!
  • She kissed her kids good night.

content.text: Excuse me, where is the restroom? Thank you very {{c1::much}}!
content.explanation: 'Much' in 'thank you very much' is an adverbial modifier indicating a high degree of gratitude.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde está el baño? ¡Muchas gracias!
content.usage:
Pattern: thank you very much / thanks a lot
  • Thank you very much for your help.
  • Thank you very much, you are very kind.

content.text: Hi Maria! {{c1::Long time no see}}! How have you been?
content.explanation: 'Long time no see' is a very common informal greeting used when meeting someone you have not seen for a significant period of time.
content.spanish: ¡Hola María! ¡Cuánto tiempo sin verte! ¿Cómo has estado?
content.usage:
Pattern: long time no see / haven't seen you in ages
  • Long time no see! You look great.
  • I haven't seen you in ages, how are things?

content.text: {{c1::Welcome back}}! We missed you at the office last week.
content.explanation: 'Welcome back' is a warm greeting used when someone returns to a place or group after an absence.
content.spanish: ¡Bienvenido de vuelta! Te extrañamos en la oficina la semana pasada.
content.usage:
Pattern: welcome back / glad to have you back
  • Welcome back from your vacation!
  • Welcome back, we missed having you on the team.

content.text: Hey David! {{c1::How's it going}}? Are you ready for the meeting?
content.explanation: 'How's it going?' is a casual, informal way to ask someone how they are doing, equivalent to 'How are you?'
content.spanish: ¡Hola David! ¿Cómo te va? ¿Estás listo para la reunión?
content.usage:
Pattern: how's it going / how are things
  • How's it going? — Pretty good, thanks!
  • How's it going with the new project?

content.text: I have to run to a meeting, but let's catch up later. {{c1::Catch you later}}!
content.explanation: 'Catch you later' is a very casual, friendly farewell that means 'see you later' or 'goodbye for now'.
content.spanish: Tengo que correr a una reunión, pero pongámonos al día luego. ¡Hasta luego! (nos vemos)
content.usage:
Pattern: catch you later / talk soon
  • Catch you later! I'm heading out.
  • Gotta go — catch you later!

content.scenario: 🛒 Grocery and Shopping — Finding Items: 'Where can I find...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask a store clerk where an item is located, ask: {{c1::"Excuse me, where can I find the olive oil?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪk·skjuːz mi, wɛr kə·naɪ faɪnd ðə...'
Rules applied:
• can I → /kə·naɪ/ (schwa reduction on 'can' + linking consonant to vowel)
• find the → /faɪn·ðə/ (consonant elision: the /d/ of 'find' is dropped before the consonant /ð/)

🧠 'Where can I find...' is the most natural way to locate items in a retail or grocery store.
content.spanish: 'Disculpe, ¿dónde puedo encontrar el aceite de oliva?' — Pregunta clásica para ubicar productos en una tienda. Fonéticamente, 'can I' se reduce a 'kə-naɪ' y la /d/ de 'find' desaparece antes de 'the'.
content.usage:
• "Excuse me, where can I find the paper towels?"
  • "Excuse me, where can I find the gluten-free section?"

content.scenario: 🛒 Grocery and Shopping — Checking out: 'Paper or plastic?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
At the grocery checkout counter, the clerk will ask: "Do you want paper or plastic?" To respond, say: {{c1::"I brought my own bags, thanks." or "Paper is fine."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'peɪ·pər·ər plæs·tɪk?'
• paper or → /peɪ·pər·ər/ (linking C+V: final /r/ of 'paper' links to 'or' which reduces to schwa /ər/)

🧠 The question refers to the type of shopping bags. In many regions, bringing your own reusable bags is standard, and paper/plastic bags carry a small fee.
content.spanish: '¿Quiere bolsas de papel o de plástico?' -> 'I brought my own bags' / 'Paper is fine'. Al pagar, el cajero te preguntará 'paper or plastic?'. Fonética: 'paper or' se enlaza reduciendo 'or' a '/ər/'.
content.usage:
• "I'll take paper, please."
  • "Actually, I brought my own bags, thanks."

content.scenario: 🎨 Hobbies and Interests — Asking: 'What do you do for fun?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask someone about their hobbies in a casual conversation, say: {{c1::"What do you do for fun when you're not working?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'wʌ·dʒə duː fər fʌn wɛ·njər nɑt wər·kɪŋ?'
Rules applied:
• what do you → /wʌ·dʒə/ (Yod Coalescence: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ + schwa reduction)
• for → /fər/ (schwa reduction)
• when you're → /wɛ·njər/ (linking consonant to glide /j/)

🧠 Avoid asking 'What are your hobbies?' as it sounds like an interview question. 'What do you do for fun?' is much more conversational.
