content.spanish: '¿Qué haces para divertirte cuando no estás trabajando?' — Pregunta natural sobre pasatiempos. En inglés rápido, 'what do you' colapsa a 'wah-juh', 'for' se reduce a 'fer' y 'when you're' se pronuncia 'wen-jer'.
content.usage:
• "What do you do for fun on the weekends?"
  • "So, what do you do for fun? Do you hike?"

content.scenario: 🎨 Hobbies and Interests — Expressing Passion: 'I'm really into...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To express that you enjoy a hobby, use: {{c1::"I'm really into photography lately." or "I've been getting into bouldering."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'aɪm rɪ·li ɪn·tə...'
• into → /ɪn·tə/ (schwa reduction on 'to')
• getting into → /gɛ·ɾɪ·ŋɪn·tə/ (Flap T on 'getting' + linking /ŋ/ to 'into')

🧠 'I'm into [X]' is the standard colloquial replacement for 'I like [X]'. 'I've been getting into [X]' implies it is a new interest.
content.spanish: 'Me gusta mucho la fotografía últimamente.' / 'He estado metiéndome en la escalada.' — Expresar interés de forma nativa. 'I'm into' = me gusta/interesa. Fonéticamente, la preposición 'to' se debilita a schwa y 'getting' usa Flap T.
content.usage:
• "I'm really into cooking Italian food lately."
  • "I've been getting into running since the spring started."

content.text: Explaining to the landlord: "The basement has accumulated {{c1::mold and mildew}} due to the high {{c2::moisture levels}}, so we need a dehumidifier."
content.explanation: Mold and mildew refer to fungal growths caused by dampness. **Moisture** refers to wetness or condensation in the air.
content.spanish: Explicándole al propietario: "El sótano ha acumulado moho y humedad debido a los altos niveles de humedad, por lo que necesitamos un deshumidificador".
content.usage:
Pattern: accumulate mold due to high moisture / run a dehumidifier
  • High moisture levels in the bathroom will cause mold and mildew.
  • We bought a dehumidifier to keep the basement dry.

content.text: Troubleshooting the setup: "The smart lock has a {{c1::sync issue}} with the hub, so the door sensor doesn't {{c2::trigger the security routine}}."
content.explanation: Sync issue refers to synchronization failure. **Trigger a routine** means to automatically start a sequence of pre-programmed actions.
content.spanish: Resolviendo problemas de la configuración: "La cerradura inteligente tiene un problema de sincronización con el concentrador, por lo que el sensor de la puerta no activa la rutina de seguridad".
content.usage:
Pattern: have a sync issue / trigger a routine based on [event]
  • My phone is having a sync issue with the server.
  • Connecting the sensor will trigger the alarm routine.

content.text: Discussing the rent: "We need to pay the first month's utilities and the {{c1::security deposit}} before we can inspect the {{c2::floor plan}}."
content.explanation: Security deposit is a sum of money paid in advance to protect the landlord against damage. **Floor plan** is the scale diagram showing room layouts.
content.spanish: Discutiendo el alquiler: "Debemos pagar los servicios del primer mes y el depósito de seguridad antes de poder inspeccionar el plano de distribución de la casa".
content.usage:
Pattern: pay a security deposit / check the floor plan
  • The landlord returned our security deposit when we moved out.
  • Let's study the floor plan to decide where to put the sofa.

content.text: Instructing the barista: "If your {{c1::grind size}} is too coarse, it causes {{c2::channeling}}, resulting in a watery crema."
content.explanation: Grind size determines the speed of water flow. **Channeling** occurs when water finds paths of least resistance through the coffee puck instead of extracting evenly.
content.spanish: Instruyendo al barista: "Si el tamaño de la molienda es demasiado grueso, causa canalización, lo que resulta en una crema aguada".
content.usage:
Pattern: adjust the grind size / prevent channeling during extraction
  • If the grind size is too fine, the espresso will extract too slowly.
  • Tamping the coffee evenly prevents channeling.

content.text: Explaining the game: "This is a {{c1::turn-based game}} with specific card mechanics, including a roll of dice to resolve any {{c2::tie-breaker}}."
content.explanation: Turn-based denotes a gameplay mechanic where players take turns. **Tie-breaker** is the rule or action that determines a winner when scores are equal.
content.spanish: Explicando el juego: "Este es un juego por turnos con mecánicas de cartas específicas, que incluye un lanzamiento de dados para resolver cualquier desempate".
content.usage:
Pattern: turn-based game / resolve a tie-breaker
  • Civilization is a famous turn-based strategy game.
  • The team won the match in a sudden-death tie-breaker.

content.text: Don't forget to {{c1::take out the trash}} before the garbage truck arrives tomorrow morning.
content.explanation: To 'take out the trash' means to carry refuse or garbage containers from inside the house to the outdoor collection spot.
content.spanish: No olvides sacar la basura antes de que llegue el camión de la basura mañana por la mañana.
content.usage:
Pattern: take out the trash / take out the rubbish
  • It is your turn to take out the trash tonight.
  • I took out the trash before going to bed.

content.text: I need to {{c1::run some errands}}, including dropping off the dry cleaning and buying groceries.
content.explanation: To 'run errands' means to take short trips to complete daily tasks or chores outside the house (e.g. post office, grocery store, cleaners).
content.spanish: Necesito hacer algunos mandados/recados, incluyendo dejar la ropa en la tintorería y comprar víveres.
content.usage:
Pattern: run errands / do errands
  • I have to run some errands this afternoon, so I'll be out of the office.
  • She spent the whole Saturday running errands.

content.text: The kitchen sink is leaking, so I need to {{c1::call a plumber}} to fix it.
content.explanation: To 'call a plumber' is a standard collocation for contacting a professional who installs and repairs pipes, fixtures, and water systems.
content.spanish: El fregadero de la cocina gotea, así que necesito llamar a un plomero para que lo arregle.
content.usage:
Pattern: call a plumber / hire a plumber
  • We had to call a plumber to unclog the drain.
  • The plumber charged $100 just to inspect the pipes.

content.text: We need to {{c1::stock up on}} toilet paper and paper towels this weekend.
content.explanation: To 'stock up on' means to buy a large quantity of something (usually household supplies or food) to keep for future use.
content.spanish: Necesitamos abastecernos de papel higiénico y toallas de papel este fin de semana.
content.usage:
Pattern: stock up on [goods]
  • People stocked up on water and canned food before the storm.
  • I always stock up on coffee when it goes on sale.

content.text: Could you please {{c1::put away the groceries}} in the pantry?
content.explanation: To 'put away groceries' means to place purchased food items in their proper storage locations (fridge, freezer, cabinets, pantry) after shopping.
content.spanish: ¿Podrías por favor guardar las compras en la despensa?
content.usage:
Pattern: put away the groceries
  • Help me put away the groceries before the ice cream melts.
  • She was busy putting away the groceries.

content.text: I need to do a load of laundry and hang the clothes to dry on the {{c1::clothesline}}.
content.explanation: A 'clothesline' is a cord or wire stretched between two points outdoors or indoors, used for hanging wet clothes to dry.
content.spanish: Necesito lavar una carga de ropa y colgar la ropa para que se seque en el tendedero.
content.usage:
Pattern: hang clothes on the clothesline
  • The clothesline broke under the weight of the wet blankets.
  • I hung my shirts out on the clothesline.

content.text: Could you please sweep the kitchen floor and {{c1::mop it}} afterwards?
content.explanation: To 'mop' the floor means to clean it using a mop (a bundle of thick strings or sponge on a stick) and water/cleanser.
content.spanish: ¿Podrías por favor barrer el piso de la cocina y trapearlo después?
content.usage:
Pattern: mop the floor / mop it up
  • Make sure you mop the floor after cooking.
  • I mopped up the spilled milk.

content.text: The roof is leaking during heavy rain; we need to hire a contractor to {{c1::patch it up}}.
content.explanation: To 'patch up' is a phrasal verb meaning to repair or fix something in a quick, temporary, or modular way.
content.spanish: El techo gotea durante la lluvia intensa; necesitamos contratar a un contratista para que lo parche/repare.
content.usage:
Pattern: patch [something] up / repair
  • We patched up the hole in the drywall.
  • The plumber patched up the leaking pipe until we could replace it.

content.text: Don't forget to lock the back door and close all the windows before you {{c1::head out}}.
content.explanation: To 'head out' is an informal phrasal verb meaning to leave a place or begin a journey.
content.spanish: No olvides cerrar con llave la puerta trasera y cerrar todas las ventanas antes de salir.
content.usage:
Pattern: head out / leave / set off
  • We are planning to head out early tomorrow morning.
  • I'm heading out to the grocery store.

content.text: We need to replace the burnt-out {{c1::light bulb}} in the hallway.
content.explanation: A 'light bulb' is the glass bulb that fits into a lamp or fixture to provide light electrically.
content.spanish: Necesitamos reemplazar la bombilla/foco quemado en el pasillo.
content.usage:
Pattern: replace the light bulb / LED bulb
  • We bought energy-efficient LED light bulbs for the living room.
  • The light bulb in my desk lamp just went out.

content.text: Could you help me clear the table and {{c1::load the dishwasher}}?
content.explanation: To 'load the dishwasher' means to place dirty plates, glasses, and utensils inside the dishwashing machine before running a cycle.
content.spanish: ¿Podrías ayudarme a limpiar la mesa y llenar el lavavajillas?
content.usage:
Pattern: load the dishwasher / unload the dishwasher
  • It is easier to load the dishwasher if you rinse the plates first.
  • She unloaded the dishwasher this morning.

content.text: We should tidy up the living room before our guests {{c1::arrive}}.
content.explanation: To 'arrive' is the standard verb meaning to reach a destination, commonly used for visitors or packages.
content.spanish: Deberíamos ordenar la sala antes de que lleguen nuestros invitados.
content.usage:
Pattern: guests arrive / package arrives
  • The guests arrived at 7:00 PM.
  • We are waiting for the package to arrive.

content.text: I need to water the plants in the garden and trim the {{c1::lawn}}.
content.explanation: A 'lawn' is an area of grass in a garden or park that is kept cut short and neat.
content.spanish: Necesito regar las plantas del jardín y podar el césped.
content.usage:
Pattern: mow the lawn / trim the lawn / cut the grass
  • He spent the afternoon mowing the lawn.
  • Our lawn needs to be watered regularly during the summer.

content.text: The battery in the smoke detector is dying; it keeps making a {{c1::chirping sound}}.
content.explanation: A 'chirping sound' is a short, high-pitched repeating noise made by electronic devices (like smoke detectors) to warn that the battery is low.
content.spanish: La batería del detector de humo se está agotando; sigue haciendo un pitido intermitente.
content.usage:
Pattern: chirping sound / beep sound
  • The smoke detector was making an annoying chirping sound all night.
  • Replace the battery to stop the chirping sound.

content.text: Please put the leftovers in a container and store them in the {{c1::fridge}}.
content.explanation: 'Fridge' is the very common abbreviation for refrigerator, the appliance used to keep food and drinks cold.
content.spanish: Por favor, ponga las sobras en un recipiente y guárdelas en el refrigerador.
content.usage:
Pattern: put in the fridge / keep in the refrigerator
  • Is there any milk left in the fridge?
  • Store the meat in the fridge immediately.

content.text: I have a reservation under the name Perez, and I'd like to check if my room is {{c1::ready for check-in}}.
content.explanation: ready for check-in means that the hotel room has been cleaned, inspected, and is fully prepared for the guest to enter and receive their key.
content.spanish: Tengo una reservación a nombre de Pérez, y me gustaría verificar si mi habitación está lista para registrarme.
content.usage:
Pattern: ready for check-in
  • Is my room ready for check-in? Standard check-in is at 3 PM.
  • The front desk agent told me the room is not ready yet.

content.text: I have a reservation under the name Colin, and I'd like to {{c1::check in}}.
content.explanation: To 'check in' at a hotel is the formal process of registering, showing ID, providing payment, and receiving room keys.
content.spanish: Tengo una reservación bajo el nombre Colin, y me gustaría registrarme.
content.usage:
Pattern: check in / check-in desk
  • We can't check in until 3:00 PM.
  • Where is the check-in counter?

content.text: Does the room rate {{c1::include breakfast}}, or is there an extra charge?
content.explanation: To 'include breakfast' means that breakfast is provided for free as part of the price paid for the hotel room (often called 'complimentary breakfast').
content.spanish: ¿La tarifa de la habitación incluye desayuno, o hay un cargo extra?
content.usage:
Pattern: include breakfast / complimentary breakfast
  • Does this booking include breakfast?
  • Our stay includes a free buffet breakfast.

content.text: Could we request a {{c1::late check-out}} for tomorrow at 2:00 PM?
content.explanation: A 'late check-out' is an arrangement allowing hotel guests to stay in their room past the standard checkout time (typically 11:00 AM or 12:00 PM).
content.spanish: ¿Podríamos solicitar una salida tardía para mañana a las 2:00 PM?
content.usage:
Pattern: late check-out / request a late check-out
  • We requested a late check-out because our flight is at night.
  • The hotel charged us a fee for late check-out.

content.text: Excuse me, does the hotel offer a {{c1::shuttle service}} to the airport?
content.explanation: A 'shuttle service' is a regular, complimentary transport service (usually a van or bus) provided by a hotel to move guests to and from specific locations like airports or tourist sights.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿el hotel ofrece servicio de transporte gratuito al aeropuerto?
content.usage:
Pattern: shuttle service / airport shuttle
  • The hotel shuttle service runs every half hour.
  • We took the free shuttle service to the beach.

content.text: My key card is not working; could you please {{c1::re-key it}} for me?
content.explanation: To 're-key' a card means to reprogram the electronic magnetic strip or RFID chip on a hotel key card so that it opens the guest's room door.
content.spanish: Mi tarjeta llave no funciona; ¿podría volver a programarla para mí?
content.usage:
Pattern: re-key the card / reprogram the key
  • My card got deactivated near my phone, so they had to re-key it.
  • The receptionist re-keyed my card in seconds.

content.text: Could you please send some extra towels and toiletries up to {{c1::our room}}?
content.explanation: To 'send up to a room' is the standard phrasal expression used by guests when requesting room service or housekeeping to deliver items directly to their hotel room.
content.spanish: ¿Podría por favor enviar algunas toallas y artículos de tocador adicionales a nuestra habitación?
content.usage:
Pattern: send [items] up to our room
  • Could you send a bottle of water up to room 405?
  • Housekeeping will send the pillows up to your room.

content.text: Is there a {{c1::safe deposit box}} in the room to store our valuables?
content.explanation: A 'safe deposit box' (or in-room safe) is a small, secure electronic metal box inside a hotel room closet where guests can store passports, cash, and jewelry.
content.spanish: ¿Hay caja fuerte en la habitación para guardar nuestros objetos de valor?
content.usage:
Pattern: safe deposit box / room safe
  • I put my passport in the safe deposit box.
  • Make sure you lock the safe deposit box before leaving.

content.text: I need to make a phone call, is there free {{c1::local calling}} from the room?
content.explanation: 'Local calling' refers to telephone calls made to numbers within the same city or local calling area, which hotels often offer for free.
content.spanish: Necesito hacer una llamada telefónica, ¿hay llamadas locales gratuitas desde la habitación?
content.usage:
Pattern: local calling / long-distance calls
  • Local calling is free, but long-distance calls will be charged to your room.
  • Does this phone support free local calling?

content.text: We'd like to {{c1::leave our bags}} at the front desk until our room is ready.
content.explanation: To 'leave bags' (or store luggage) means to temporarily place your suitcases in the hotel's secure holding room when arriving before check-in or departing after check-out.
content.spanish: Nos gustaría dejar nuestras maletas en recepción hasta que nuestra habitación esté lista.
content.usage:
Pattern: leave our bags / store our luggage
  • Can we leave our bags here while we explore the city?
  • You can leave your bags with the bellhop.

content.text: I'd like to request a room with a view of the ocean, if you have any {{c1::availability}}.
content.explanation: 'Availability' refers to the state of being vacant, unoccupied, or ready for booking (e.g. empty hotel rooms).
content.spanish: Me gustaría solicitar una habitación con vista al océano, si tienen alguna disponibilidad.
content.usage:
Pattern: room availability / check availability
  • Do you have any availability for this weekend?
  • Please check the room availability on the database.

content.text: Is there a gym or a fitness center inside the hotel that guests can use {{c1::free of charge}}?
content.explanation: 'Free of charge' is a professional phrase meaning without any payment, cost, or fee required.
content.spanish: ¿Hay un gimnasio o centro de acondicionamiento físico dentro del hotel que los huéspedes puedan usar de forma gratuita?
content.usage:
Pattern: free of charge / complimentary
  • The hotel provides Wi-Fi free of charge.
  • Parking is available free of charge for guests.

content.text: Excuse me, what time does the pool close, and do we need to bring our own {{c1::towels}}?
content.explanation: A 'towel' is a sheet of absorbent fabric or paper used for wiping or drying your body or a surface.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿a qué hora cierra la piscina, y necesitamos llevar nuestras propias toallas?
content.usage:
Pattern: bath towels / pool towels
  • Housekeeping left some fresh towels in the bathroom.
  • You can collect pool towels at the reception desk.

content.text: Could you arrange a wake-up call for tomorrow morning at {{c1::6:00 AM}}?
content.explanation: A 'wake-up call' is a telephone call arranged by a hotel guest with the front desk to wake them up at a specific time in the morning.
content.spanish: ¿Podría programar una llamada de despertador para mañana a las 6:00 AM?
content.usage:
Pattern: wake-up call / request a wake-up call
  • I set a wake-up call for 5:00 AM to catch my early flight.
  • Did you receive your wake-up call?

content.text: I need to print some flight tickets; is there a computer available in the {{c1::business center}}?
content.explanation: A 'business center' is a room in a hotel equipped with computers, printers, scanners, and internet access for guests to use.
content.spanish: Necesito imprimir algunos boletos de avión; ¿hay una computadora disponible en el centro de negocios?
content.usage:
Pattern: business center / office room
  • The business center is open 24 hours a day on the second floor.
  • I printed my boarding pass in the business center.

content.text: Is the tap water in the bathroom safe to drink, or should we use {{c1::bottled water}}?
content.explanation: 'Bottled water' is purified drinking water packaged in plastic or glass bottles, commonly provided in hotel rooms.
content.spanish: ¿El agua del grifo en el baño es segura para beber, o deberíamos usar agua embotellada?
content.usage:
Pattern: bottled water / tap water
  • We always buy bottled water when traveling abroad.
  • The hotel provides two complimentary bottles of water daily.

content.text: We need to check out tomorrow morning; can we pay the bill in cash or should we keep it on the {{c1::credit card}}?
content.explanation: A 'credit card' is a plastic card issued by a bank allowing the holder to purchase goods or services on credit.
content.spanish: Necesitamos hacer el check-out mañana por la mañana; ¿podemos pagar la cuenta en efectivo o la dejamos en la tarjeta de crédito?
content.usage:
Pattern: credit card / debit card
  • We charged our hotel stay to our corporate credit card.
  • Do you accept this credit card?

content.text: Could you call a taxi for us to take us to the train station after we {{c1::check out}}?
content.explanation: To 'check out' at a hotel is the formal process of leaving the room, settling the bill, and returning room keys.
content.spanish: ¿Podría pedir un taxi para nosotros que nos lleve a la estación de tren después de que hagamos el check-out?
content.usage:
Pattern: check out / check-out time
  • The checkout time is 11:00 AM.
  • We checked out of the hotel early in the morning.

content.text: Does this hotel provide laundry service, and how long does it take to get the clothes {{c1::returned}}?
content.explanation: To get clothes 'returned' is the standard terminology for receiving laundered or dry-cleaned garments back from the hotel service.
content.spanish: ¿Este hotel ofrece servicio de lavandería, y cuánto tiempo toma tener la ropa de vuelta?
content.usage:
Pattern: returned laundry / dry cleaning delivery
  • If I drop off my suit now, when will it be returned?
  • Your laundry will be returned by tomorrow evening.

content.text: Pardon me, where is the emergency exit located on this {{c1::floor}}?
content.explanation: A 'floor' (or level/story) refers to the entire level of a building, containing rooms and hallways on that horizontal plane.
content.spanish: Disculpe, ¿dónde se encuentra la salida de emergencia en este piso?
content.usage:
Pattern: on this floor / ground floor / top floor
  • Our room is located on the fourth floor.
  • Housekeeping is currently cleaning the third floor.

content.scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Checking In: 'I have a reservation under...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
At check-in, tell the receptionist: {{c1::"Hi, I have a reservation under the name Colin."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'haɪ, aɪ hæ·və rɛz·ər·veɪ·ʃən ʌn·dər ðə neɪm...'
Rules applied:
• have a → /hæ·və/ (C+V linking + schwa)
• reservation under → /rɛz·ər·veɪ·ʃə·nʌn·dər/ (linking final /n/ to /ʌ/)

🧠 'Under the name [X]' is the standard linguistic pattern for reservations in hotels, restaurants, and event bookings.
content.spanish: 'Hola, tengo una reservación a nombre de Colin.' — El patrón 'under the name [X]' es la estructura por excelencia para indicar a nombre de quién está la reserva. Fonéticamente, 'have a' y 'reservation under' se entrelazan de manera continua.
content.usage:
• "I have a reservation under the name Colin, check-in for three nights."
  • "Could you look up a reservation under 'Colin'?"

content.scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Requesting Amenities: 'Is breakfast included?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To clarify hotel services, ask: {{c1::"Is breakfast included, and what's the Wi-Fi password?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪz brɛk·fəs·tɪn·kluː·dɪd, ən wʌ·sðə waɪ·faɪ pæs·wərd?'
• breakfast included → /brɛk·fəs·tɪn·kluː·dɪd/ (linking final /t/ of 'breakfast' to initial /ɪ/ of 'included')
• and what's → /ən·wʌts/ (weak /d/ deletion in 'and' + schwa)

🧠 Always ask this at the desk to avoid hidden charges. Note the /t/ migration from 'breakfast' to 'included'.
content.spanish: '¿Está incluido el desayuno, y cuál es la contraseña del Wi-Fi?' — Pregunta esencial de hotel. La /t/ final de 'breakfast' se enlaza directamente con la /ɪ/ de 'included' creando un sonido fluido.
content.usage:
• "Is breakfast included in the room rate?"
  • "What time does breakfast start?"

content.scenario: 🏨 Hotel English — Room Complaints: 'The AC isn't working'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To report a problem politely but effectively, tell the front desk: {{c1::"Excuse me, the AC in my room isn't working properly."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪk·skjuːz mi, ði eɪ·siː ɪn maɪ ruːm ɪ·zn̩t wər·kɪŋ prɑː·pər·li'
• isn't → /ɪ·zn̩t/ or /ɪ·zn̩/ (the final /t/ is often elided or glottalized before consonants)
• 'AC' is the most common abbreviation for air conditioning.

🧠 Politeness anchors: Lead with 'Excuse me' to reduce defensiveness in staff.
content.spanish: 'Disculpe, el aire acondicionado de mi habitación no funciona correctamente.' — Informar sobre fallas de forma asertiva. Fonética: la /t/ en 'isn't' generalmente desaparece o se frena antes de 'working'.
content.usage:
• "Excuse me, the heater isn't working properly."
  • "The hot water isn't coming out."

content.text: I’m just {{c1::pulling your leg}}.
content.explanation: This idiom means joking or teasing someone playfully, not being serious.
content.spanish: Solo estoy bromeando contigo.
content.usage:
Pattern: pulling your leg
  • Relax, I’m pulling your leg.
  • He was pulling my leg about the surprise.

content.text: That joke really {{c1::cracked me up}}.
content.explanation: To crack someone up means to make them laugh a lot. It is common in casual conversation.
content.spanish: Ese chiste me hizo reír mucho.
content.usage:
Pattern: crack me up / made me laugh
  • Your story cracked me up.
  • That was hilarious.

content.text: I’m {{c1::on cloud nine}} after hearing the good news.
content.explanation: This idiom describes being extremely happy or excited.
content.spanish: Estoy en la cima de la felicidad después de escuchar la buena noticia.
content.usage:
Pattern: on cloud nine
  • She was on cloud nine after the promotion.
  • We were on cloud nine all weekend.

content.text: It’s not {{c1::rocket science}}.
content.explanation: This idiom means that something is not very difficult. It is often said humorously.
content.spanish: No es ciencia de cohetes.
content.usage:
Pattern: not rocket science
  • It’s not rocket science; just follow the steps.
  • This part is not rocket science.

content.text: We’re all in the {{c1::same boat}} here.
content.explanation: This idiom means that everyone is dealing with the same situation or problem.
content.spanish: Todos estamos en la misma situación.
content.usage:
Pattern: in the same boat
  • We’re all in the same boat with this deadline.
  • Everyone was in the same boat after the outage.

content.text: That was the {{c1::last straw}}.
content.explanation: This idiom refers to the final problem that makes someone lose patience or stop tolerating something.
content.spanish: Eso fue lo que terminó de colmar el vaso.
content.usage:
Pattern: the last straw
  • Missing the deadline was the last straw.
  • That was the last straw for me.

content.scenario: Idioms: A Lot on My Plate 🍽️
content.text: I’ve got a lot on my {{c1::plate}} right now.
content.explanation: This idiom means someone has many responsibilities or tasks to manage.
content.spanish: Tengo muchas cosas que atender en este momento.
content.usage:
Pattern: have a lot on your plate
  • I have a lot on my plate this week.
  • She’s got too much on her plate.

content.scenario: Idioms: You Can Say That Again 🙌
content.text: You can say that {{c1::again}}.
content.explanation: This phrase means that someone strongly agrees with what was just said.
content.spanish: Eso es exactamente lo que pienso.
content.usage:
Pattern: you can say that again
  • You can say that again.
  • I completely agree — you can say that again.

content.scenario: 💬 Idiom: 'Cost an arm and a leg'
content.text: The idiom '{{c1::It cost an arm and a leg}}' means something was extremely expensive.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation (stress-timed rhythm):
'it COS·tan·ARM·an·uh·LEG'
• Stress falls on COST, ARM, LEG — content words
• 'an' → /ən/ (schwa); 'and a' → /ən·ə/ both reduced
• The idiom always takes past tense: 'cost' (not 'costs')

🧠 Origin: Possibly from 18th century portrait painting — full-body portraits with arms and legs visible cost more.
content.spanish: 'Cost an arm and a leg' = costar un ojo de la cara / costar un riñón. Pronunciación: 'it COS-tan-ARM-an-uh-LEG' — el ritmo acompasado del inglés comprime 'an', 'and', 'a' en sonidos rápidos de schwa. Solo en pasado: 'cost' (no 'costs').
content.usage:
• "That hotel in New York? It cost an arm and a leg."
  • "I need a new laptop but they're gonna cost an arm and a leg right now."
  • "Synonyms: 'a small fortune', 'a fortune', 'an absolute steal' (the opposite — very cheap)"
  • Context: casual conversation, never formal writing

content.text: {{c1::"I'm on the fence"}} means you haven't decided yet — you're neutral or undecided about something.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation: 'I'm on the fence' → /aɪm·ɑn·ðuh·fɛns/
• 'on the' → /ɑn·ðuh/ — schwa in 'the'
• 'the fence' → linking: /ðuh·fɛns/ — 'the' and 'fence' link smoothly

🧠 Visual image: sitting on a fence between two sides, not committing to either. Perfect for non-committal social responses.

Related phrases:
• 'I could go either way.' — equally fine with both options
• 'I haven't made up my mind yet.'
• 'I'm torn.' — you want both, or feel conflicted
content.spanish: 'I'm on the fence' = estoy indeciso / no me he decidido todavía. Pronunciación: /aɪm·ɑn·ðuh·fɛns/ — 'the' se reduce a schwa /ðuh/. Relacionado: 'I could go either way' (cualquier opción me parece bien), 'I'm torn' (estoy dividido).
content.usage:
• "Should I take the job offer?" — "Honestly, I'm still on the fence."
  • "Are you coming tonight?" — "I'm on the fence — depends how tired I am."
  • "What do you think about [political topic]?" — "I'm kind of on the fence on that one."

content.text: {{c1::"That rings a bell"}} means something sounds familiar — you vaguely remember it.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation: 'that rings a bell' → /ðæ·trɪŋz·ə·bɛl/
• Resyllabification (C+V linking): 'that' + 'rings' → the /t/ of 'that' attaches to 'rings': /ðæt·rɪŋz/ — smooth, no pause
• 'a' → /ə/ (schwa: unstressed article)

🧠 Usage context: When someone mentions a name, place, or event and you half-remember it. Response to 'Do you know Maria from accounting?' — 'That rings a bell — does she have short hair?'
content.spanish: 'That rings a bell' = eso me suena / me resulta familiar. Pronunciación: /ðæ·trɪŋz·ə·bɛl/ — Resyllabification: la /t/ de 'that' se une a 'rings'. La 'a' se reduce a schwa. Opuesto: 'I'm drawing a blank' = no recuerdo nada.
content.usage:
• "Her name rings a bell but I can't place her."
  • "That movie rings a bell — was it from the 90s?"
  • Stronger: 'That sounds very familiar.' / 'I think I know what you mean.'
  • Negative: 'That doesn't ring a bell at all.' / 'I'm drawing a blank.'

content.text: {{c1::"Hit the ground running"}} means to start something immediately and with full energy — no ramp-up period.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation (stress-timed):
'HIT·ðuh·GROUND·RUN·nɪŋ'
• Stress: HIT, GROUND, RUN — content words
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa)
• 'ground' → /ɡraʊnd/ — Dark L precedes? No L here, but unstressed middle gets compressed

🧠 Common in professional contexts: new job, new project, new semester. Often heard in hiring: 'We need someone who can hit the ground running from day one.'
content.spanish: 'Hit the ground running' = empezar de lleno / arrancar con todo desde el inicio. Muy común en contextos profesionales: contratación, proyectos nuevos. Ritmo: HIT-ðuh-GROUND-RUN-nɪŋ — el inglés acompasado por el estrés comprime 'the' a schwa.
content.usage:
• "She was new but really hit the ground running on the project."
  • "I want to hit the ground running when I start Monday."
  • Professional context: Interview answer: 'I've worked with this tech stack before, so I can hit the ground running.'
  • Synonym: 'jump right in', 'dive in head first'

content.text: {{c1::"Bite the bullet"}} means to endure a painful or difficult situation because you have no other choice.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation: 'bite the bullet' → /baɪt·ðuh·ˈbʊ·lɪt/
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa)
• 'bullet' → /ˈbʊ·lɪt/ — Flap T: the /t/ in 'bullet' between /ʊ/ and /ɪ/ is a tap [ɾ]: /ˈbʊ·lɪt/ (some speakers use full /t/ here, but many use a soft tap in fast speech)

🧠 Origin: Soldiers before anesthesia would literally bite a bullet during surgery to endure pain.

Modern use: Any unpleasant-but-necessary action: expensive car repair, difficult conversation, boring but required task.
content.spanish: 'Bite the bullet' = tragarse el sapo / aguantar lo que toca. Para situaciones dolorosas pero necesarias. Pronunciación: /baɪt·ðuh·ˈbʊ·lɪt/ — 'the' → schwa, 'bullet' puede tener Flap T en habla rápida.
content.usage:
• "I hate going to the dentist but I just had to bite the bullet and make an appointment."
  • "Sometimes you just gotta bite the bullet and do the thing you've been putting off."
  • Synonyms: 'suck it up', 'take the hit', 'grin and bear it', 'just do it'

content.text: {{c1::"I'm swamped"}} and {{c1::"I'm slammed"}} both mean extremely busy — too much work and not enough time.
content.explanation:
Pronunciation:
• 'I'm swamped' → /aɪm·swɑːmpt/ — final consonant cluster: /-mpt/
• 'I'm slammed' → /aɪm·slæmd/

🧠 Register: Both very colloquial — fine in work Slack/email, not in formal writing.
• 'I'm swamped' = full like a swamp (overwhelmed by volume)
• 'I'm slammed' = hit hard by work load (more intense)
• 'Buried' = also common: 'I'm totally buried this week'
• Professional alternative: 'I have a packed schedule this week.'
content.spanish: 'I'm swamped' / 'I'm slammed' = estoy hasta el cuello / tengo demasiado trabajo. Muy coloquiales — perfectas para Slack o emails informales. Gradaciones: busy → hectic → swamped → slammed → underwater (la peor).
content.usage:
• "Hey, want to grab lunch?" — "Can't today, I'm slammed."
  • "I'd love to help but I'm completely swamped with the launch."
  • "My inbox is swamped — I'll get back to you tomorrow."
  • Gradations: busy → hectic → swamped → slammed → underwater (worst)

content.text: In American English, '{{c1::grab}}' replaces many verbs: grab a coffee (get), grab your coat (take), grab a seat (sit down), grab lunch (eat quickly).
content.explanation:
'Grab' is one of the most versatile colloquial verbs in American English. It implies speed, informality, and action.

Pronunciation: 'grab a' → /ɡræ·bə/ — linking: /b/ + /ə/ → smooth tap
• 'Let me grab that' → /lɛ·mi·ɡræb·ðæt/ (chunks + linking)
• 'Lemme' → /ˈlɛ·mi/ — lexical chunk (Let me → lemme)

🧠 In a café: 'Can I grab you a coffee?' = would you like me to get you a coffee? It's an offer, not a question about grabbing things.
content.spanish: 'Grab' en inglés americano reemplaza a 'get', 'take', 'eat', 'drink', 'sit' — un verbo multiusos informal. 'grab a coffee' = tomar un café, 'grab lunch' = ir a comer, 'grab a seat' = siéntate. Pronunciación: 'grab a' → /ɡræ·bə/ — enlace suave.
content.usage:
• "Want to grab lunch later?" = let's eat lunch together
  • "Let me grab my keys." = I'll go get my keys
  • "Can you grab that for me?" = can you take/fetch that?
  • "Grab a seat wherever you like." = sit anywhere
  • "I'm gonna grab a quick shower." = take a quick shower
  • "Can I grab you something to drink?" = can I get you something?

content.text: {{c1::"No worries"}} and {{c1::"No biggie"}} are casual ways to say 'it's fine' or 'don't apologize — it's not a problem.'
content.explanation:
Pronunciation:
• 'No worries' → /noʊ·ˈwɜː·riːz/ — Dark L? No L here, but note: 'worries' has the schwa-like /ɜː/ (stressed)
• 'No biggie' → /noʊ·ˈbɪ·ɡi/ — Flap T in 'biggie'? No /t/ here — but the /-gi/ ending is very soft

🧠 Context: Used when someone apologizes for something minor. Very Australian in origin but completely standard in the US now.

Register escalation:
• Very casual: 'No worries!' / 'No biggie!' / 'All good!'
• Neutral: 'It's fine.' / 'Not a problem.'
• Formal: 'Please don't worry about it.' / 'It's not an issue at all.'
content.spanish: 'No worries' / 'No biggie' = no hay problema / no te preocupes / está todo bien. Muy coloquial. Escala: No biggie! (muy casual) → It's fine. (neutral) → Please don't worry about it. (formal). 'Don't sweat it' = otro idioma: no te estreses por eso.
content.usage:
• "Oh sorry, I'm late!" — "No worries! We just got here."
  • "I forgot to send that email." — "No biggie, I'll resend it."
  • "All good!" = common alternative (very American/Australian)
  • "Don't sweat it." = idiom: don't stress about it

content.scenario: 🎭 Jokes and Humor — Telling a Joke: 'Did you hear the one about...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
A common way to introduce a joke in conversational English is: {{c1::"Did you hear the one about the three-legged dog?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'dɪ·dʒə hɪr ðə wʌ·nə·baʊt...'
Rules applied:
• Did you → /dɪ·dʒə/ (Yod Coalescence: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ + schwa)
• one about → /wʌ·nə·baʊt/ (linking C+V: final /n/ of 'one' migrates to initial /ə/ of 'about')

🧠 'Did you hear the one about...' is a formulaic conversational opener specifically used for jokes. It signals that a narrative punchline is coming.
content.spanish: '¿Escuchaste el chiste del perro de tres patas?' — 'Did you hear the one about...' es una frase hecha para introducir un chiste. En inglés rápido 'did you' experimenta coalescencia de Yod (/dɪ-dʒə/) y 'one about' se une por consonante+vocal.
content.usage:
• "Did you hear the one about the programmer and the coffee machine?"
  • "Did you hear the one about the guy who walked into a bar?"

content.scenario: 🎭 Jokes and Humor — The Punchline Transition
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To transition to the twist or resolution of a story/joke, native speakers say: {{c1::"So it turns out, the guy was in the wrong house the whole time!"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'soʊ·ɪ·tər·nz·aʊt...'
• it turns out → /ɪ·tərnz·aʊt/ (linking C+V: 'turns' ends in /z/, 'out' starts with /aʊ/ → /tərnz·aʊt/)
• turns out means 'as a result of investigation, it was discovered that...'

🧠 'So it turns out...' is an essential narrative marker to reveal the unexpected truth of a story.
content.spanish: 'Resulta que...' — Se usa para revelar el giro inesperado en una anécdota o chiste. Fonética: 'it turns out' se vincula acumulando los sonidos de consonante final a vocal inicial.
content.usage:
• "So it turns out, she was his boss, not his coworker!"
  • "So it turns out, I had my shirt on backwards all day."

content.scenario: 🎭 Jokes and Humor — Reacting to a bad joke
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When reacting to a dad joke or a pun, a native speaker will often roll their eyes and say sarcastically: {{c1::"Oh, very funny. You're a real comedian." or "Alright, good one."}}
content.explanation:
Pronunciation and Intonation:
• 'Good one' → /gʊd wʌn/ (usually spoken with a falling flat intonation to signal mild sarcasm)
• 'comedian' → /kəˈmiː.di.ən/ (schwa on first syllable)

🧠 Sarcastically acknowledging bad puns is a core element of Anglo social humor. A flat tone is critical to show you are playing along but not genuinely amazed.
content.spanish: 'Ah, muy gracioso. Un verdadero comediante.' u 'Ok, qué buena.' — Respuestas irónicas a chistes malos o juegos de palabras. La entonación plana es clave para denotar sarcasmo.
content.usage:
• "That was terrible, but alright, good one."
  • "Don't quit your day job!" (standard joke-rejection idiom)

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To invite someone out casually, the default opening question is: {{c1::"Are you free to grab dinner sometime next week?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ər·jə friː tə græb dɪ·nər sʌm·taɪm nɛks wiːk?'
Rules applied:
• are you → /ər·jə/ (schwa reduction on 'you')
• free to → /friː·tə/ (schwa reduction on 'to')
• grab → /græb/ (colloquial usage for 'get/eat')
• next week → /nɛks wiːk/ (consonant elision: final /t/ of 'next' is elided before the consonant /w/)

🧠 'Are you free...' is low-pressure. Combining it with 'grab' and 'sometime' makes the invitation sound casual and easy to accept.
content.spanish: '¿Estás libre para ir a cenar alguna vez la próxima semana?' — Invitación informal. Fonética: 'are you' se reduce a 'er-juh', 'to' a 'tuh', 'grab' es el verbo coloquial y en 'next week' se omite la /t/ final de 'next' (/nɛks/).
content.usage:
• "Are you free to grab a coffee tomorrow afternoon?"
  • "Are you free to hang out this weekend?"

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To cancel or postpone plans politely because of an unexpected event, say: {{c1::"Hey, sorry but something came up. Can we reschedule?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'heɪ, sɑː·ri bət sʌm·θɪŋ keɪ·mʌp. kə·nwi ri·skɛ·dʒuːl?'
• something came up → /sʌm·θɪŋ keɪ·mʌp/ (linking C+V: final /m/ of 'came' links to 'up')
• can we → /kə·nwi/ (schwa reduction on 'can')

🧠 'Something came up' is a vague but polite idiomatic phrase used to signal that an unforeseen situation requires your attention, without oversharing personal details.
content.spanish: 'Oye, lo siento pero surgió algo. ¿Podemos reprogramar?' — Cancelar o posponer planes cortésmente. 'Something came up' es un modismo para indicar un imprevisto sin dar detalles incómodos. Fonéticamente, 'came up' se enlaza y 'can' se reduce a schwa.
content.usage:
• "I'm really sorry to do this, but something came up at work. Can we reschedule for Thursday?"
  • "Something came up last minute, so I won't be able to make it tonight."

content.text: Can I get a cheeseburger and {{c1::a side of}} fries, please?
content.explanation: a side of is the standard, natural collocation to order an additional side portion of food alongside your main dish.
content.spanish: ¿Me da una hamburguesa con queso y una porción de papas fritas, por favor?
content.usage:
Pattern: a side of [food]
  • I'd like a steak with a side of mashed potatoes.
  • Can I get a side of ranch dressing?

content.text: We are ready to order: I'll have the steak, and can I get the dressing {{c1::on the side}}?
content.explanation: To get a sauce or dressing 'on the side' means it should be served in a small separate cup rather than poured directly over the food.
content.spanish: Estamos listos para pedir: Tomaré el filete, ¿y me puede dar el aderezo aparte/a un lado?
content.usage:
Pattern: on the side / sauce on the side
  • I'd like a salad with vinaigrette on the side.
  • Can I get the gravy on the side?

content.text: Excuse me, I'm allergic to nuts; does this dish {{c1::contain any nuts}}?
content.explanation: To 'contain nuts' is a crucial health expression used to check if tree nuts or peanuts are used in the ingredients or preparation of a food item.
content.spanish: Disculpe, soy alérgico a las nueces; ¿este plato contiene nueces?
content.usage:
Pattern: contain nuts / nut-free
  • Does the pesto sauce contain any nuts?
  • Please let the chef know that my food must not contain any nuts.

content.text: Can we get a {{c1::pitcher of tap water}} for the table, please?
content.explanation: A 'pitcher of tap water' is a large container of municipal drinking water provided for free in US/UK restaurants, as opposed to bottled water.
content.spanish: ¿Nos puede traer una jarra de agua del grifo para la mesa, por favor?
content.usage:
Pattern: pitcher of tap water / jug of water
  • Just a pitcher of tap water is fine, thank you.
  • We always order a pitcher of tap water.

content.text: I'd like my steak cooked {{c1::medium-rare}}, please.
content.explanation: 'Medium-rare' is the standard cooking level for meat where the center is warm and red, generally preferred by culinary experts for texture and flavor.
content.spanish: Me gustaría mi filete término medio (tres cuartos), por favor.
content.usage:
Pattern: medium-rare / rare / medium / well-done
  • I prefer my ribeye cooked medium-rare.
  • He ordered his burger well-done.

content.text: Excuse me, we've been waiting for our food for thirty minutes; could you {{c1::check on our order}}?
content.explanation: To 'check on an order' is a polite way to ask a server to go to the kitchen and find out why the food is delayed.
content.spanish: Disculpe, hemos estado esperando nuestra comida por treinta minutos; ¿podría revisar nuestro pedido?
content.usage:
Pattern: check on our order / verify the status
  • Could you please check on our order? It's taking a bit long.
  • I'll go to the kitchen to check on your order right away.

content.text: This isn't what I ordered; I asked for the grilled chicken, not the {{c1::fried chicken}}.
content.explanation: This is a polite yet direct way to notify a server that they have brought the wrong dish to your table.
content.spanish: Esto no es lo que pedí; pedí el pollo a la parrilla, no el pollo frito.
content.usage:
Pattern: this isn't what I ordered / wrong dish
  • Excuse me, this isn't what I ordered; I wanted a salad.
  • I apologize, I'll take this back and bring you the correct plate.

content.text: We are done eating, could you please {{c1::bring us the check}}?
content.explanation: To 'bring the check' (or bring the bill) is the standard request to pay for the meal at the end of dining.
content.spanish: Hemos terminado de comer, ¿podría por favor traernos la cuenta?
content.usage:
Pattern: bring us the check / can we get the bill
  • Excuse me, can you bring us the check when you have a moment?
  • He asked the waiter to bring the check.

content.text: Do you have any vegan options, or can this dish be {{c1::made vegan}}?
content.explanation: To make a dish 'vegan' means modifying the recipe to exclude all animal products (meat, dairy, eggs, honey).
content.spanish: ¿Tienen alguna opción vegana, o este plato se puede hacer vegano?
content.usage:
Pattern: made vegan / vegan-friendly
  • Can this pasta be made vegan if you remove the cheese?
  • Is there a vegan alternative for this sauce?

content.text: Can we split the bill {{c1::evenly between}} the three of us?
content.explanation: To 'split the bill evenly' means to divide the total cost of a restaurant meal into equal portions among the diners.
content.spanish: ¿Podemos dividir la cuenta en partes iguales entre los tres?
content.usage:
Pattern: split the bill / go Dutch
  • Let's split the bill evenly to keep things simple.
  • We decided to split the bill instead of paying separately.

content.text: Could we get a menu to look at the desserts, or do you have a {{c1::dessert tray}}?
content.explanation: A 'dessert tray' is a platter or cart displaying physical samples of available desserts, shown to diners at the end of a meal to encourage them to order.
content.spanish: ¿Podríamos pedir un menú para ver los postres, o tiene una bandeja de postres?
content.usage:
Pattern: dessert tray / dessert menu
  • The waiter brought a dessert tray showing cheesecakes and chocolate tarts.
  • I was too full until I saw the dessert tray.

content.text: Excuse me, I ordered my salad without onions, but this one has {{c1::onions on it}}.
content.explanation: This is a polite way to point out that a kitchen error occurred, specifically regarding a requested ingredient omission (onions).
content.spanish: Disculpe, pedí mi ensalada sin cebolla, pero esta tiene cebollas encima.
content.usage:
Pattern: without [ingredient], but it has [ingredient] on it
  • I asked for no cheese, but this burger has cheese on it.
  • It has nuts on it; I cannot eat it.

content.text: Is it possible to replace the french fries with a side salad instead, without any {{c1::extra charge}}?
content.explanation: An 'extra charge' (or surcharge) is an additional cost added to the bill for modifying a dish or requesting premium substitutions.
content.spanish: ¿Es posible reemplazar las patatas fritas con una ensalada de acompañamiento, sin ningún cargo adicional?
content.usage:
Pattern: extra charge / additional fee
  • Substituting almond milk has an extra charge of 50 cents.
  • There is no extra charge for extra ice.

content.text: We'd like to order a bottle of red wine; which one do you recommend to pair with the {{c1::steak}}?
content.explanation: 'Steak' is a high-quality slice of beef (like ribeye or sirloin), commonly paired with red wine (like Cabernet Sauvignon) in fine dining.
content.spanish: Nos gustaría pedir una botella de vino tinto; ¿cuál nos recomienda para maridar con el filete?
content.usage:
Pattern: steak pair with [wine] / T-bone steak
  • We ordered a medium-rare steak with a side of asparagus.
  • Does this steak come with mushroom sauce?

content.text: Could we get some extra napkins and a clean fork for the table, please? One of ours {{c1::fell on the floor}}.
content.explanation: To 'fall on the floor' (or drop on the floor) is the standard phrase used to explain why you need a replacement utensil from the server.
content.spanish: ¿Podríamos pedir algunas servilletas adicionales y un tenedor limpio para la mesa, por favor? Uno de los nuestros se cayó al suelo.
content.usage:
Pattern: fell on the floor / dropped on the ground
  • I need a clean knife; this one fell on the floor.
  • The glass fell on the floor and shattered.

content.text: The soup is a bit cold, could you please have the kitchen {{c1::reheat it}} for me?
content.explanation: To 'reheat' food means to warm it up again, commonly requested in restaurants if a dish arrives lukewarm.
content.spanish: La sopa está un poco fría, ¿podría por favor hacer que la cocina la recaliente por mí?
content.usage:
Pattern: reheat the food / warm it back up
  • Could you reheat this coffee? It has gone completely cold.
  • I'll ask the chef to reheat your soup right away.

content.text: Can we get these leftovers packed in a box to take home, or do you have {{c1::to-go containers}}?
content.explanation: A 'to-go container' (or doggy bag) is a disposable plastic or paper box provided by a restaurant so guests can carry their leftovers home.
content.spanish: ¿Podemos llevarnos lo que sobró empacado en una caja a casa, o tienen envases para llevar?
content.usage:
Pattern: to-go containers / doggy bag / takeout box
  • We couldn't finish our pizzas, so we asked for to-go containers.
  • Do you charge for to-go containers?

content.text: Is service included in the bill, or is the tip {{c1::left to our discretion}}?
content.explanation: 'Left to our discretion' is a formal phrase meaning that a choice or amount (like a gratuity/tip) is not mandatory and is decided entirely by the customer.
content.spanish: ¿El servicio está incluido en la cuenta, o la propina queda a nuestra discreción?
content.usage:
Pattern: left to [someone's] discretion / optional choice
  • The tip is left to the client's discretion.
  • The dress code is left to the manager's discretion.

content.text: Do you accept credit cards, or is this restaurant {{c1::cash-only}}?
content.explanation: 'Cash-only' describes a business that only accepts physical money (banknotes/coins) for payment, refusing credit cards or digital transfers.
content.spanish: ¿Aceptan tarjetas de crédito, o este restaurante es de solo efectivo?
content.usage:
Pattern: cash-only business / cash payment
  • This coffee shop is cash-only, but there is an ATM nearby.
  • I couldn't buy lunch because the food truck was cash-only.

content.text: Can we book a table for six people for this Friday night at {{c1::8:00 PM}}?
content.explanation: To 'book a table' means to make a reservation in advance at a restaurant.
content.spanish: ¿Podemos reservar una mesa para seis personas para este viernes por la noche a las 8:00 PM?
content.usage:
Pattern: book a table / make a reservation
  • I need to book a table for our anniversary dinner.
  • It is hard to book a table at this restaurant on weekends.

content.scenario: English A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In English, how do you say 'First person singular'? {{c1::I am}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'First person singular' en English? I am
content.usage: Term: I am | Meaning: First person singular

content.scenario: English A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In English, how do you say 'Second person singular/plural'? {{c1::You are}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Second person singular/plural' en English? You are
content.usage: Term: You are | Meaning: Second person singular/plural

content.scenario: English A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In English, how do you say 'Third person singular'? {{c1::He/She/It is}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Third person singular' en English? He/She/It is
content.usage: Term: He/She/It is | Meaning: Third person singular

content.scenario: English A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In English, how do you say 'First person plural'? {{c1::We are}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'First person plural' en English? We are
content.usage: Term: We are | Meaning: First person plural

content.scenario: English A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
content.text: In English, how do you say 'Third person plural'? {{c1::They are}}
content.explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
content.spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Third person plural' en English? They are
content.usage: Term: They are | Meaning: Third person plural

content.text: She {{c1::is}} a software engineer, and her brother {{c2::is}} a database administrator.
content.explanation: 'Is' is the singular third-person form (he/she/it) of the verb 'to be' in the present tense.
content.spanish: Ella es ingeniera de software, y su hermano es administrador de bases de datos.
content.usage:
Pattern: he/she/it is [noun/adjective]
  • She is a developer.
  • It is a beautiful day.

content.text: They {{c1::are}} from Mexico, but they currently live and work in the United States.
content.explanation: 'Are' is the plural present tense form (they/we/you) of the verb 'to be'.
content.spanish: Ellos son de México, pero actualmente viven y trabajan en los Estados Unidos.
content.usage:
Pattern: they/we/you are [noun/adjective]
  • They are from Madrid.
  • We are ready.

content.text: I {{c1::am}} a student, and I want to learn English to study abroad next year.
content.explanation: 'Am' is the first-person singular present tense form (I) of the verb 'to be'.
content.spanish: Soy estudiante, y quiero aprender inglés para estudiar en el extranjero el próximo año.
content.usage:
Pattern: I am [noun/adjective]
  • I am happy.
  • I am a software tester.

content.text: We {{c1::are}} happy to help you with your project; please let us know what you need.
content.explanation: 'Are' is used with the plural subject pronoun 'we' to describe a current state or identity.
content.spanish: Estamos felices de ayudarle con su proyecto; por favor háganos saber lo que necesita.
content.usage:
Pattern: we are [noun/adjective]
  • We are glad you came.
  • We are members of the team.

content.text: It {{c1::is}} cold outside today, so make sure you wear a warm jacket.
content.explanation: 'Is' is used with the pronoun 'it' when referring to weather, temperature, or non-human objects.
content.spanish: Hace frío afuera hoy (Está frío afuera hoy), así que asegúrate de usar una chaqueta abrigada.
content.usage:
Pattern: it is [adjective/noun]
  • It is hot today.
  • It is a database issue.

content.text: You {{c1::are}} our guest, so please make yourself at home.
content.explanation: 'Are' is the present tense verb used with both singular and plural second-person pronoun 'you'.
content.spanish: Eres nuestro invitado, así que por favor siéntete como en casa.
content.usage:
Pattern: you are [noun/adjective]
  • You are a helpful teammate.
  • You are welcome.

content.text: He {{c1::is}} tired after a long day of work, so he is going to sleep early tonight.
content.explanation: 'Is' is the singular form of 'to be' used with the third-person masculine pronoun 'he'.
content.spanish: Él está cansado después de un largo día de trabajo, por lo que va a dormir temprano esta noche.
content.usage:
Pattern: he is [adjective/noun]
  • He is the project manager.
  • He is busy right now.

content.text: They {{c1::are}} late for the meeting, we need to start without them.
content.explanation: 'Are' is the present tense plural form of 'to be' used with the third-person plural pronoun 'they'.
content.spanish: Ellos llegan tarde a la reunión, necesitamos comenzar sin ellos.
content.usage:
Pattern: they are [adjective/noun]
  • They are behind schedule.
  • They are developers.

content.text: She {{c1::is}} my sister, and her name is Maria.
content.explanation: 'Is' is the singular present tense form of 'to be' used with the third-person feminine pronoun 'she'.
content.spanish: Ella es mi hermana, y su nombre es María.
content.usage:
Pattern: she is [noun/adjective]
  • She is our team leader.
  • She is very helpful.

content.text: I {{c1::am}} very happy to meet you; you {{c2::are}} a very kind person.
content.explanation: 'Am' is the first-person singular present tense, and 'are' is the second-person singular present tense form of 'to be'.
content.spanish: Estoy muy feliz de conocerte; eres una persona muy amable.
content.usage:
Pattern: I am [adjective] / you are [noun]
  • I am happy to know you.
  • You are a wonderful friend.

content.text: The teacher called {{c1::him}} and asked {{c2::her}} to present their homework first.
content.explanation: 'Him' and 'her' are third-person singular object pronouns, used as the object of a verb or preposition (not the subject).
content.spanish: El maestro llamó a él y le pidió a ella que presentaran su tarea primero.
content.usage:
Pattern: call him / thank her / help them
  • She told him to wait outside.
  • I asked her to review my code.

content.text: This laptop is {{c1::mine}}, and that tablet on the desk is {{c2::yours}}.
content.explanation: 'Mine' is the first-person singular possessive pronoun, and 'yours' is the second-person possessive pronoun. They replace 'my [noun]' and 'your [noun]' respectively.
content.spanish: Esta laptop es mía, y esa tableta en el escritorio es tuya.
content.usage:
Pattern: it's mine / it's yours
  • The red cup is mine; the blue one is yours.
  • Is this seat yours?

content.text: I {{c1::am not}} late; the meeting {{c2::is not}} until 3:00 PM.
content.explanation: The negative form of 'to be' is made by adding 'not' after the verb: 'am not', 'is not' (isn't), 'are not' (aren't).
content.spanish: No llego tarde; la reunión no es hasta las 3:00 PM.
content.usage:
Pattern: am not / is not / are not
  • He is not ready yet.
  • We are not from here.

content.text: {{c1::Are}} you the new developer? {{c2::Is}} the project manager in the office today?
content.explanation: Questions with 'to be' are formed by inverting the subject and verb: 'Are you...?' / 'Is he...?' No auxiliary verb is needed.
content.spanish: ¿Eres el nuevo desarrollador? ¿Está el gerente de proyecto en la oficina hoy?
content.usage:
Pattern: Are you [noun]? / Is he [adjective]?
  • Is she the project lead?
  • Are they on the same team?

content.text: Please introduce {{c1::yourself}} to the team; we are all very excited to meet you.
content.explanation: 'Yourself' is a second-person singular reflexive pronoun, used when the subject and object of the sentence are the same person.
content.spanish: Por favor, preséntate al equipo; todos estamos muy emocionados de conocerte.
content.usage:
Pattern: myself / yourself / himself / herself / themselves
  • She introduced herself to the new team members.
  • I did the work myself.

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When calling someone and a third party answers, ask: {{c1::"Hi, is Colin there?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'haɪ, ɪz kɑ·lɪn ðɛr?'
Rules applied:
• is Colin there → /ɪz kɑ·lɪn ðɛr/ (linking consonant to consonant)

🧠 'Is [Name] there?' is the standard informal way to ask to speak to someone on the phone. It is friendlier than 'May I speak to [Name]?' which is reserved for formal customer service calls.
content.spanish: 'Hola, ¿está Colin?' — Forma informal estándar para pedir hablar con alguien al teléfono. Es mucho más cercana que 'May I speak to...' (reservado para atención al cliente formal).
content.usage:
• "Hi, is Colin there? I'm returning his call."
  • "Hi, is Sarah there by any chance?"

content.scenario: 📱 Phone and Messages — Bad Connection: 'You're breaking up'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When the connection is bad and you can't hear the other person clearly, say: {{c1::"Hey, you're breaking up. Can you repeat that?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'heɪ, jər breɪ·kɪ·ŋʌp. kə·njuː rɪ·piːt ðæt?'
• breaking up → /breɪ·kɪ·ŋʌp/ (linking C+V: final /ŋ/ of 'breaking' links to 'up')
• Can you → /kə·njuː/ or /kə·njə/ (schwa reduction + palatal linking)

🧠 'Breaking up' is a specific phrasal verb meaning that the audio signal is cutting in and out. Compare with 'You cut out' (the audio stopped completely or the call dropped).
content.spanish: 'Oye, te estás cortando. ¿Puedes repetir eso?' — Indicar una mala señal telefónica. 'Breaking up' se refiere a que la voz va y viene. Fonéticamente, 'breaking up' se une de forma continua y 'can' se reduce a schwa.
content.usage:
• "I'm losing you, you're breaking up. I'll call you back."
  • "You're breaking up a bit, let me move to another room."

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When arriving at a restaurant, say: {{c1::"Hi, table for two please." or "Do you have anything available?"}}
content.explanation:
Pronunciation: 'table for two' → /ˈteɪ·bəl·fər·tuː/
• 'for' → /fər/ (schwa — extremely common reduction of 'for' in speech)
• 'two' → /tuː/ (stays stressed — it's content, not function)

Common host responses:
• 'Right this way.' — follow them
• 'It'll be about 20 minutes.' → /ɪ·ɾl·bi·ə·baʊ·ˈtwɛn·i·ˈmɪ·nɪts/ — Flap T in 'It'll'!
• 'Do you have a reservation?' → /duː·juh·hæ·və·rɛz·ər·ˈveɪ·ʃən/ — schwa everywhere
content.spanish: 'Table for two please.' → 'teɪ·bəl·fər·tuː·pliːz' — 'for' se reduce a /fər/ (schwa), muy común en inglés coloquial. 'It'll be about 20 minutes' → /ɪ·ɾl·bi·ə·baʊ·twɛ·ni·mɪ·nɪts/ — Flap T en 'it'll'.
content.usage:
• "Table for two, please — we don't have a reservation."
  • "Is there a wait?" → /ɪz·ðɛr·ə·weɪt/
  • "Could we sit outside if possible?"
  • "Can we get a booth?" → /kə·nwi·gɛ·tuh·buːð/ — linking: 'get a' → 'ge-tuh'
  • "We're actually three now — can you add a chair?"

content.scenario: 🍽️ Restaurant — Reading the menu and asking questions
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
The most useful question to a server is: {{c1::"What do you recommend?" or "What's your most popular dish?"}}
content.explanation:
Native pronunciation:
• 'What do you recommend?' → /wʌ·djuh·rɛ·kə·mɛnd/
• 'What do you' → yod coalescence: 'do you' → /djuh/
• 'recommend' → /rɛ·kə·ˈmɛnd/ — stress on last syllable, schwa in middle

Menu vocabulary:
• Entrée = main dish (US), not starter like in French
• Appetizer = starter / first course
• Side = side dish (fries, salad, etc.)
• A la carte = ordering items separately
• Prix fixe = set price menu (often 2-3 courses)
content.spanish: 'What do you recommend?' → /wʌ·djuh·rɛ·kə·mɛnd/ — Yod coalescence: 'do you' → /djuh/. Vocabulario: Entrée = plato principal (en EE.UU., NO es entrante como en francés). Appetizer = entrante. Side = guarnición. Prix fixe = menú de precio fijo.
content.usage:
• "What's the special today?" → /wʌ·sðuh·ˈspɛ·ʃəl·tuh·deɪ/
  • "Is this spicy?" (always ask before ordering Thai/Indian)
  • "Does this come with a side?"
  • "Can I substitute the fries for a salad?" → /kə·naɪ·ˈsʌb·stɪ·tjuːt/
  • "What does 'house dressing' mean?" → the restaurant's own recipe dressing

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
When ordering food, say: {{c1::"I'll have the [dish], please."}} — NOT 'I want the...' or 'Give me the...'
content.explanation:
'I'll have the...' → /aɪl·hæv·ðuh.../
• 'I'll' = I will (contracted) — very smooth /aɪl/
• 'have the' → linking: 'have' ends in /v/, 'the' starts with /ð/ → /hæv·ðuh/

🧠 'I want the...' is grammatically fine but sounds demanding. 'Give me the...' without 'please' is rude in American English.

Specifying doneness:
• Rare → red center / Blue → basically raw
• Medium rare → pink center (most popular for steak)
• Medium → slightly pink
• Well done → fully cooked through
content.spanish: 'I'll have the...' → /aɪl·hæv·ðuh.../ — la forma más cortés y natural de pedir comida. 'I want' es un poco exigente. 'Give me' sin 'please' es grosero en inglés americano. Para el punto de la carne: rare (rojo), medium rare (rosado — el más popular), medium (ligeramente rosado), well done (completamente cocido).
content.usage:
• "I'll have the salmon, please." /aɪl·hæv·ðuh·ˈsæm·ən·pliːz/
  • "And I'll have the burger, medium."
  • "Can I get the pasta without the onions?"
  • "I'll start with the Caesar salad."
  • "We'll share the appetizer." /wiːl·ʃɛr·ðuh·æ·pɪ·taɪ·zər/

content.scenario: 🍽️ Restaurant — During the meal: server check-ins
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
The server will ask: "How is everything?" or "How's everything tasting?" The natural reply is: {{c1::"Everything's great, thank you!" or "It's delicious — compliments to the chef."}}
content.explanation:
Server question pronunciation:
• 'How's everything?' → /haʊz·ˈɛv·ri·θɪŋ/
• 'everything' → /ˈɛv·ri·θɪŋ/ — schwa absorbed, rapid middle syllable

If something is wrong:
• 'Actually, this is a bit under-seasoned.' (diplomatic)
• 'I'm sorry, but could I get this heated up a bit more?' (food came cold)
• 'Excuse me, I think this might not be what I ordered.'

🧠 Americans rarely complain directly — they hedge with 'actually', 'I think', 'a bit'.
content.spanish: Cuando el mesero pregunta '¿Cómo está todo?', la respuesta natural es 'Everything's great, thank you!' o 'It's delicious.' Si algo está mal, los americanos son indirectos: 'Actually, I think this might be...' — nunca acusan directamente. 'Compliments to the chef' = felicidades al cocinero.
content.usage:
• If great: "Everything's wonderful, thanks!"
  • If okay: "Yeah, it's pretty good!" (honest but positive)
  • If food is wrong: "Actually, I ordered the salmon, not the tuna."
  • If service is slow: "Whenever you get a chance, could we get some more water?"
  • Best compliment: "Compliments to the chef — this is incredible."

content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To get the bill in the US say: {{c1::"Can we get the check, please?"}} — NOT 'bill' (that's British English).
content.explanation:
Pronunciation: 'Can we get the check?' → /kən·wi·gɛt·ðuh·tʃɛk/
• 'Can we' → /kən·wi/ (schwa: 'can' → /kən/)
• 'get the' → /gɛt·ðuh/ (schwa: 'the' → /ðuh/)

Splitting the bill:
• 'Can we split this?' → each person pays half
• 'Can we get separate checks?' → each person pays their own
• 'I've got this one.' → you're treating (paying for the whole table)
• 'Let me get this.' → same, I'm paying

US tipping at sit-down restaurant: 18-20% minimum (standard), 25% for great service.
content.spanish: En EE.UU. di 'the check' (no 'the bill' — eso es inglés británico). 'Can we get the check?' → /kən·wi·gɛt·ðuh·tʃɛk/ — 'can' y 'the' se reducen a schwa. Propina en restaurante con servicio de mesa: 18-20% mínimo. 'I've got this one' = yo invito (pago yo todo).
content.usage:
• "Can we get the check, whenever you have a second?"
  • "We'd like to split it three ways, please."
  • "Can I get a receipt for that?" → /fər·ðæt/ (schwa on 'for')
  • "Keep the change." (cash)
  • "This one's on me." = I'm paying for everyone

content.scenario: 🗳️ Small Talk: Politics — Neutral disagreement: 'I see your point, but...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To express disagreement politely during political or social discussions, say: {{c1::"I see where you're coming from, but I look at it a bit differently."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'aɪ siː wɛr jər kʌ·mɪŋ frɑm, bə daɪ lʊ·kə·dɪt ə bɪt dɪ·frənt·li'
Rules applied:
• where you're → /wɛr·jər/ (linking consonant to glide)
• but I → /bə·daɪ/ (Flap T cross-boundary: /t/ of 'but' acts as flap [ɾ] between /ʌ/ and /aɪ/)
• look at it → /lʊ·kə·dɪt/ (linking + Flap T: /k/ of 'look' links to 'at', and /t/ of 'at' flaps before 'it')

🧠 Politeness strategy: validate the other person's perspective ('I see where you're coming from') before offering your contrasting view to avoid creating hostility.
content.spanish: 'Entiendo tu punto de vista, pero yo lo veo un poco diferente.' — Discrepar de manera educada en temas sociales/políticos. Fonética: 'but I' se escucha como 'ba-dai' (Flap T) y 'look at it' se enlaza como 'lu-ka-dit'.
content.usage:
• "I see where you're coming from, but I look at the tax policy a bit differently."
  • "I see your point, but don't you think that might be too expensive?"

content.scenario: 🗳️ Small Talk: Politics — Dodging political topics
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To politely avoid discussing politics in a social setting, use the phrase: {{c1::"Honestly, I try to avoid talking about politics if I can help it."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɑː·nəs·tli, aɪ traɪ tə ə·vɔɪd tɑː·kɪ·ŋə·baʊt...'
• try to → /traɪ·tə/ (schwa reduction on 'to')
• talking about → /tɑː·kɪ·ŋə·baʊt/ (linking C+V: /ŋ/ links to 'about')
• if I can → /ɪ·faɪ kən/ (linking /f/ to /aɪ/ + schwa on 'can')

🧠 'If I can help it' is a fixed idiom meaning 'if it is possible to avoid'. It is a highly respected boundary setting statement in US culture.
content.spanish: 'Honestamente, trato de evitar hablar de política si puedo evitarlo.' — Establecer un límite cortés ante temas polarizantes. 'If I can help it' es una frase hecha para decir 'si está en mis manos evitarlo'. Fonéticamente, 'talking about' se enlaza y 'can' se reduce a schwa.
content.usage:
• "Honestly, I try to avoid talking about politics if I can help it. Let's talk about something happier!"
  • "I try to steer clear of political debates at dinner tables if I can help it."

content.scenario: 🌤️ Small Talk: Weather — 'Nice day, isn't it?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
A classic small talk opener on a sunny day is: {{c1::"Beautiful day today, isn't it?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'bjuː·ɾə·fəl deɪ tə·deɪ, ɪ·zn̩·tɪt?'
Rules applied:
• beautiful → /bjuː·ɾə·fəl/ (Flap T within word + schwa)
• today → /tə·deɪ/ (schwa reduction of 'to')
• isn't it → /ɪ·zn̩·tɪt/ (linking final /n/ and /t/ of 'isn't' to 'it')

🧠 Tag questions ('isn't it?', 'hot out there, huh?') are social invitations designed to get the other person to agree, establishing an immediate rapport.
content.spanish: 'Hermoso día hoy, ¿verdad?' — Un clásico iniciador de conversación trivial. La palabra 'beautiful' tiene un Flap T en la 't', 'today' se reduce a 'tuh-day' y el tag question 'isn't it' se articula como 'is-nit'.
content.usage:
• "Beautiful day today, isn't it? Finally got some sun."
  • "Nice weather we're having, huh?"

content.scenario: 🌤️ Small Talk: Weather — Complaining about heat/cold
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To complain about extreme temperatures casually, say: {{c1::"It's freezing out there!" / "It's boiling today!"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'ɪts friː·zɪ·ŋaʊ·ðɛr!'
• freezing out → /friː·zɪ·ŋaʊt/ (linking consonant to vowel: final /ŋ/ links to 'out')
• boiling → /ˈbɔɪ·lɪŋ/ (pronounced without the 'g' in very casual speech: /ˈbɔɪ·lɪn/)

🧠 'Freezing' replaces 'very cold' and 'boiling' or 'scorching' replaces 'very hot' in colloquial English.
content.spanish: '¡Hace un frío helado afuera!' / '¡Está hirviendo hoy!' — Alternativas coloquiales para describir temperaturas extremas. Fonéticamente, el sonido /ŋ/ de 'freezing' se enlaza directamente con 'out'.
content.usage:
• "Man, it is freezing out there! I should've worn a thicker coat."
  • "It's absolutely boiling today, the AC is struggling to keep up."

content.scenario: 📅 Small Talk: Yesterday — Asking about the weekend: 'What did you get up to?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask a coworker about their weekend or yesterday casually, use the phrasal verb: {{c1::"What did you get up to over the weekend?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'wʌ·dʒə gɛ·tʌp·tə...'
Rules applied:
• what did you → /wʌ·dʒə/ (Yod Coalescence: /d/ + /j/ → /dʒ/ + schwa reduction)
• get up → /gɛ·tʌp/ (Flap T cross-boundary: /t/ of 'get' is flapped between /ɛ/ and /ʌ/ → /gɛ·ɾʌp/)
• to → /tə/ (schwa reduction)

🧠 'Get up to' is a very common colloquial phrasal verb meaning 'to do' (specifically referring to leisure activities).
content.spanish: '¿Qué hiciste el fin de semana?' — 'Get up to' es un phrasal verb coloquial equivalente a 'hacer' en el tiempo libre. Fonética: 'what did you' colapsa a 'wah-juh', 'get up' se une por Flap T y 'to' se debilita a schwa.
content.usage:
• "What did you get up to yesterday? Anything fun?"
  • "What did you guys get up to last night?"

content.scenario: 📅 Small Talk: Yesterday — Answering: 'I just hung out'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
If you had a quiet day doing nothing special, the most natural native response is: {{c1::"Not much, I just hung out at home and took it easy."}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'nɑt mʌtʃ, aɪ dʒʌs hʌ·ŋaʊ·ɾət hoʊm ən tʊ·kɪ·ɾiː·zi'
• just hung → /dʒʌs hʌŋ/ (consonant elision: final /t/ of 'just' is omitted before 'hung')
• hung out at → /hʌ·ŋaʊ·ɾət/ (linking C+V: /ŋ/ links to 'out', and /t/ of 'out' is flapped [ɾ] before 'at')
• took it easy → /tʊ·kɪ·ɾiː·zi/ (linking + Flap T: /k/ of 'took' links to 'it', and /t/ of 'it' flaps before 'easy')

🧠 'Hung out' (phrasal verb 'hang out' in past) means spent time relaxing. 'Took it easy' means rested.
content.spanish: 'No mucho, solo pasé el rato en casa y me lo tomé con calma.' — Respuesta para un día tranquilo. Fonética: la /t/ de 'just' desaparece, 'hung out' se enlaza y se genera un Flap T en 'out at', igual que en 'took it easy' ('tu-ki-ri-zi').
content.usage:
• "I just hung out with some friends on Saturday."
  • "I spent the whole day taking it easy after a crazy work week."

content.scenario: ⚽ Sports and Fandom — Asking about a score: 'What's the score?'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask about the status of a live game, ask: {{c1::"What's the score of the game? Who's winning?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'wʌ·sðə skɔːr əv ðə geɪm?'
Rules applied:
• What's the → /wʌ·sðə/ (linking final /s/ of 'what's' to 'the' + schwa reduction)
• score of → /skɔː·rəv/ (linking C+V: final /r/ of 'score' links to 'of' /əv/)

🧠 'What's the score?' is the universal question for sports updates. Note how 'of the' reduces to /əv ðə/ where 'of' is a mere murmur.
content.spanish: '¿Cómo va el partido? ¿Quién va ganando?' — Preguntar por el marcador. Fonética: 'What's the' suena '/wʌ-sðə/' y 'score of' se une de forma que la R de 'score' viaja al sonido schwa de 'of'.
content.usage:
• "What's the score? Is Arsenal still down?"
  • "What's the score of the game? I missed the first half."

content.scenario: ⚽ Sports and Fandom — Rooting for a team: 'Root for...'
content.text:
🎧 YouGlish
To ask someone which team they support, use the verb 'root': {{c1::"Who do you root for in the Premier League?"}}
content.explanation:
Native speed: 'huː də jə ruːt fər...'
• do you → /də·jə/ (schwa reduction)
• root for → /ruːt fər/ (schwa reduction on 'for')

🧠 'Root for' is the primary American phrasal verb meaning 'to support / support a team'. British English equivalents are 'support' or 'follow'.
content.spanish: '¿A quién le vas en la Premier League?' — 'Root for' es la expresión típica en inglés americano para indicar a qué equipo apoyas (el equivalente británico es 'support'). Fonéticamente, 'do you' y 'for' se reducen a schwa.
content.usage:
• "I root for the Lakers, but my brother is a Celtics fan."
  • "Who do you root for in the NFL?"

content.text: Where can I find the {{c1::dairy aisle}}?
content.explanation: An aisle is a passage between rows of shelves in a supermarket. dairy refers to milk-based products (cheese, yogurt, butter).
content.spanish: ¿Dónde puedo encontrar el pasillo de lácteos?
content.usage:
Pattern: [category] aisle (e.g., baking aisle, produce aisle, frozen food aisle)
  • The sugar is in the baking aisle.
  • Where is the produce aisle? I need some fresh apples.

content.text: Are these apples {{c1::on sale}} this week?
content.explanation: on sale means available at a reduced price (discounted). *Do not confuse with 'for sale', which just means available to buy.
content.spanish: ¿Están estas manzanas en oferta esta semana?
content.usage:
Pattern: on sale (discounted) vs for sale (available to buy)
  • These shoes are on sale for 20% off.
  • Is that classic car for sale or is it just on display?

content.text: Is there a limit on how many items I can bring to the {{c1::express lane}}?
content.explanation: An express lane is a checkout counter reserved for customers buying only a few items (e.g. 10 or less) to speed up service.
content.spanish: ¿Hay un límite de cuántos artículos puedo llevar a la caja rápida?
content.usage:
Pattern: express lane (typically labeled '10 items or less' or '15 items or fewer')
  • I only have 3 items, so I will use the express lane.
  • You can't go there; the express lane is for quick checkouts.

content.text: At the customer service counter, the customer requested: "I'd like a {{c1::full refund}} for this tablet because it has a {{c2::faulty charging port}}."
content.explanation: Refund is the return of payment. **Faulty** means defective or not working correctly.
content.spanish: En el mostrador de servicio al cliente, el cliente solicitó: "Me gustaría un reembolso completo por esta tableta porque tiene un puerto de carga defectuoso".
content.usage:
Pattern: request a refund for [item] / faulty [part]
  • I need a full refund because the screen is cracked.
  • This laptop has a faulty battery that doesn't charge.

content.text: At the flea market, he bargained: "Is that your {{c1::best price}}? If you {{c2::throw in the vintage watch}}, we have a deal."
content.explanation: Best price is the lowest price a seller is willing to accept. **Throw in** is a phrasal verb meaning to include something extra for free.
content.spanish: En el mercado de pulgas, regateó: "¿Es ese tu mejor precio? Si incluyes el reloj vintage, tenemos un trato".
content.usage:
Pattern: throw in [item] / have a deal
  • If you throw in free shipping, I'll buy it.
  • We have a deal if you lower the price by ten dollars.

content.text: When comparing laptops, he analyzed: "There are clear {{c1::trade-offs}} in portability, but the developer version gives you the most {{c2::bang for the buck}}."
content.explanation: Trade-offs are compromises made when choosing between two conflicting features. **Bang for the buck** is an idiom meaning value for money spent.
content.spanish: Al comparar computadoras portátiles, analizó: "Hay compensaciones claras en la portabilidad, pero la versión para desarrolladores te ofrece la mejor relación calidad-precio".
content.usage:
Pattern: trade-offs between [feature] and [feature] / get the most bang for the buck
  • There are always trade-offs between battery life and processing power.
  • This budget phone offers the best bang for the buck.

content.text: On the phone with the bank, she stated: "I need to {{c1::dispute a double charge}} that appeared on my recent {{c2::billing cycle statement}}."
